syllabus_desc: Describe the general shape of the earth.
concept: The Earth isn't a perfect sphere  -  it's an oblate spheroid, meaning it's slightly flattened at the poles and bulges a bit at the equator. This happens because the Earth spins on its axis, and the centrifugal effect pushes material outward at the equator. For navigation purposes though, we treat it as a sphere. The difference is tiny (about 0.3%), but it does matter for chart projections and long-distance calculations. At PPL level, think 'slightly squashed ball' and you've got it.
exam_tip: The Earth is an oblate spheroid, not a perfect sphere. Flattened at the poles, bulging at the equator. For most navigation, we treat it as a sphere.
key_facts: Earth is an oblate spheroid (flattened at poles, bulging at equator)
Flattening caused by Earth's rotation
Equatorial diameter is about 43 km greater than polar diameter
For practical navigation, Earth is treated as a sphere
This shape affects chart projections and distance calculations
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, AIPNZ GEN

syllabus_desc: Define and identify the axis and direction of rotation.
concept: The Earth rotates around an imaginary line running from the North Pole to the South Pole  -  that's the axis of rotation. It spins from west to east, which is why the sun appears to rise in the east and set in the west. One full rotation takes approximately 24 hours. This rotation is what gives us our basic reference for direction and time, and it's the reason we have time zones. From above the North Pole, the Earth rotates anticlockwise.
exam_tip: Earth rotates west to east (anticlockwise when viewed from above North Pole). One rotation = approximately 24 hours.
key_facts: Axis runs from North Pole to South Pole
Earth rotates west to east
Anticlockwise when viewed from above the North Pole
One full rotation takes approximately 24 hours
Rotation creates the day/night cycle

syllabus_desc: Define and identify geographic and magnetic poles.
concept: The geographic poles (True North and True South) are where the Earth's axis of rotation meets the surface. They're fixed points. The magnetic poles are where the Earth's magnetic field lines converge  -  but here's the thing: they don't line up with the geographic poles. The magnetic North Pole is currently located in the Canadian Arctic, roughly 500 km from True North, and it wanders over time. This mismatch between true and magnetic north is what gives us magnetic variation, which is a big deal for NZ navigation  -  our variation is around 20-23°E depending on where you are in the country.
exam_tip: Geographic poles are fixed (axis of rotation). Magnetic poles wander and don't align with geographic poles. This difference = magnetic variation.
key_facts: Geographic (true) poles: fixed, where axis meets the surface
Magnetic poles: where magnetic field lines converge vertically
Magnetic poles do not coincide with geographic poles
Magnetic North Pole wanders over time
The angular difference between true and magnetic north = magnetic variation
NZ magnetic variation is approximately 20-23°E (and slowly increasing)
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, AIPNZ GEN 2.1, LINZ magnetic model

syllabus_desc: Define and identify the equator.
concept: The equator is the imaginary great circle around the Earth, exactly halfway between the North and South Poles. It sits at 0° latitude and divides the Earth into the Northern and Southern Hemispheres. Every point on the equator is equidistant from both poles. The equator is also the only parallel of latitude that's a great circle  -  all the others are small circles. New Zealand sits between about 34°S and 47°S latitude, so we're well south of the equator in the Southern Hemisphere.
exam_tip: Equator = 0° latitude. Only parallel that's a great circle. Divides Earth into Northern and Southern Hemispheres.
key_facts: Equator is at 0° latitude
Divides Earth into Northern and Southern Hemispheres
Equidistant from both geographic poles
The equator is a great circle (the only parallel that is)
Circumference at the equator is approximately 21,600 nm (40,000 km)

syllabus_desc: Define and identify parallels of latitude.
concept: Parallels of latitude are imaginary circles running east-west around the Earth, parallel to the equator. They're measured in degrees north or south of the equator, from 0° at the equator to 90° at the poles. Every point on a given parallel has the same latitude. Parallels get smaller as you move toward the poles  -  they're small circles (except the equator). In NZ, you'll commonly work with latitudes between about 34°S (top of the North Island) and 47°S (bottom of the South Island).
exam_tip: Parallels of latitude run east-west, measured 0° (equator) to 90° (poles). All parallels except the equator are small circles.
key_facts: Parallels run east-west, parallel to the equator
Measured in degrees north or south of the equator (0° to 90°)
All points on a parallel share the same latitude
All parallels except the equator are small circles
NZ spans approximately 34°S to 47°S

syllabus_desc: Define and identify meridians of longitude.
concept: Meridians of longitude are imaginary semi-circles (half great circles) running from pole to pole, crossing the equator at right angles. They're measured in degrees east or west of the Prime Meridian, from 0° to 180°. Unlike parallels, all meridians are the same length  -  they're all semi-great circles. Meridians converge at the poles and are widest apart at the equator. New Zealand sits at roughly 166°E to 179°E longitude, which puts us near the International Date Line.
exam_tip: Meridians run north-south from pole to pole. Measured 0° to 180° east or west of the Prime Meridian. All meridians are semi-great circles.
key_facts: Meridians run from North Pole to South Pole
Measured in degrees east or west of the Prime Meridian (0° to 180°)
All meridians are semi-great circles (same length)
Meridians converge at the poles
NZ longitude is approximately 166°E to 179°E

syllabus_desc: Define and identify the Greenwich (Prime) Meridian.
concept: The Greenwich Meridian (also called the Prime Meridian) passes through Greenwich, London, and is designated 0° longitude. It's the reference line from which all other longitudes are measured  -  east or west. The meridian directly opposite (on the other side of the Earth) is the 180° meridian, which roughly corresponds to the International Date Line. Since NZ sits at around 172-178°E, we're almost exactly on the opposite side of the Earth from Greenwich  -  which is why our time zone is UTC+12.
exam_tip: Prime Meridian = 0° longitude, passes through Greenwich, London. All longitude is measured east or west from here.
key_facts: Prime Meridian = 0° longitude at Greenwich, London
Reference for all longitude measurements
Opposite meridian is 180° (near the International Date Line)
Longitude measured 0° to 180° east or west
NZ is near 180°  -  almost antipodal to Greenwich

syllabus_desc: Define and identify latitude/longitude.
concept: Latitude and longitude together form the coordinate system that lets you pinpoint any location on Earth. Latitude tells you how far north or south you are from the equator (0° to 90°N or S), and longitude tells you how far east or west of the Prime Meridian (0° to 180°E or W). In aviation, we express coordinates in degrees and minutes  -  for example, Ardmore aerodrome is approximately 37°02'S 174°58'E. One minute of latitude always equals one nautical mile, which is extremely handy for measuring distance on charts.
exam_tip: Latitude = N/S of equator. Longitude = E/W of Prime Meridian. One minute of latitude = 1 nautical mile. Coordinates expressed in degrees and minutes.
key_facts: Latitude: 0° to 90° North or South of the equator
Longitude: 0° to 180° East or West of the Prime Meridian
Together they uniquely define any point on Earth
1 minute of latitude = 1 nautical mile
Aviation format: degrees and minutes (e.g. 37°02'S 174°58'E)

syllabus_desc: Describe the 360° method of indicating direction.
concept: In aviation, direction is measured clockwise from north using a 360° system. North is 360° (or 000°), east is 090°, south is 180°, and west is 270°. Directions are always expressed as three digits  -  so you'd say 'zero-nine-zero' for east, not just 'ninety degrees.' This three-figure group avoids any confusion between headings, bearings, and other numbers on the radio. When someone says 'turn right heading 270,' there's zero ambiguity about which way to point.
exam_tip: Direction measured clockwise from north in 360° system. Always three digits: north = 360°, east = 090°, south = 180°, west = 270°.
key_facts: Direction measured clockwise from north
Full circle = 360°
North = 360° (or 000°), East = 090°, South = 180°, West = 270°
Always expressed as a three-figure group
Eliminates ambiguity in aviation communications

syllabus_desc: Describe the earth's magnetic field.
concept: The Earth behaves like a giant bar magnet, with magnetic field lines running from the magnetic South Pole to the magnetic North Pole. Near the equator, these field lines run roughly parallel to the surface (horizontal). Near the poles, they plunge steeply into the Earth (vertical). This angle of the field lines to the horizontal is called magnetic dip (or inclination). In New Zealand, we're in the Southern Hemisphere at fairly high latitudes (34-47°S), so the magnetic dip is significant  -  the south-seeking end of a compass needle tries to dip downward. This affects compass accuracy and is why aircraft compasses have specific errors during turns and acceleration.
exam_tip: Earth's magnetic field runs pole to pole. Field lines are horizontal near the equator and nearly vertical near the poles. NZ has significant magnetic dip, which affects compass behaviour.
key_facts: Earth acts like a giant bar magnet
Magnetic field lines run from magnetic south to magnetic north
Field lines are horizontal near the equator
Field lines are nearly vertical near the magnetic poles
Angle of field lines to horizontal = magnetic dip (inclination)
NZ has significant magnetic dip due to high southern latitude

syllabus_desc: Define true north.
concept: True north is the direction along the Earth's surface toward the geographic North Pole  -  the point where the Earth's axis of rotation meets the surface. It's a fixed, unchanging reference. On your chart, the meridians of longitude all point to true north. True north is the baseline from which we measure true bearings and true tracks. It's shown on NZ VNC charts and is the starting point before you apply magnetic variation to get magnetic direction.
exam_tip: True north = direction toward the geographic North Pole. Fixed reference. Meridians point to true north.
key_facts: True north = direction to the geographic North Pole
Fixed and unchanging reference
Meridians of longitude point to true north
Basis for true tracks and true bearings on charts

syllabus_desc: Define magnetic north.
concept: Magnetic north is the direction a freely suspended magnetic compass needle points  -  toward the magnetic North Pole. Unlike true north, magnetic north is not fixed. The magnetic North Pole is currently in the Canadian Arctic and moves slowly over time (magnetic wander). The angular difference between true north and magnetic north at any location is called magnetic variation. In NZ, magnetic variation is approximately 20-23° East, meaning magnetic north is east of true north. You'll find the local variation printed on your VNC charts.
exam_tip: Magnetic north = direction a compass needle points. Differs from true north by the magnetic variation. NZ variation is roughly 20-23°E.
key_facts: Magnetic north = direction toward the magnetic North Pole
Direction a freely suspended compass needle points
Not fixed  -  the magnetic pole wanders over time
Differs from true north by magnetic variation
NZ variation is approximately 20-23°E
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, LINZ magnetic model, AIPNZ GEN 2.1

syllabus_desc: Define compass north.
concept: Compass north is the direction that your aircraft's magnetic compass actually indicates  -  and it's not quite the same as magnetic north. The difference is caused by compass deviation, which results from magnetic fields within the aircraft itself (engines, electrical systems, metal structure). Each aircraft has a deviation card near the compass showing corrections for different headings. So to go from true direction to compass direction, you apply variation first (gives magnetic), then deviation (gives compass). The mnemonic 'Variation West, Magnetic Best; Variation East, Magnetic Least' helps with the arithmetic.
exam_tip: Compass north = what your aircraft compass reads. Differs from magnetic north due to compass deviation (local magnetic interference in the aircraft).
key_facts: Compass north = direction indicated by the aircraft compass
Differs from magnetic north due to compass deviation
Deviation caused by aircraft magnetic fields (engine, electrics, structure)
Each aircraft has a deviation card showing corrections
True → Magnetic (apply variation) → Compass (apply deviation)

syllabus_desc: Define the cardinal directions of the earth.
concept: The four cardinal directions are North (N, 360°/000°), East (E, 090°), South (S, 180°), and West (W, 270°). These are the primary compass points, evenly spaced 90° apart. They form the basic framework for the compass rose. You'll use cardinal directions constantly in aviation  -  wind directions, runway orientations, and general situational awareness all rely on these four fundamental references.
exam_tip: Cardinal directions: North (360°), East (090°), South (180°), West (270°). Spaced 90° apart.
key_facts: East (E) = 090°
South (S) = 180°
West (W) = 270°
Spaced 90° apart around the compass

syllabus_desc: Define the inter-cardinal directions of the earth.
concept: The inter-cardinal directions sit halfway between the cardinal points: Northeast (NE, 045°), Southeast (SE, 135°), Southwest (SW, 225°), and Northwest (NW, 315°). Together with the four cardinal directions, they give you eight main compass points. You'll hear these in weather reports and general descriptions  -  'southwesterly winds' or 'the aerodrome is northeast of the city.' For precise navigation though, we use the three-digit bearing system rather than compass point names.
exam_tip: Inter-cardinal directions: NE (045°), SE (135°), SW (225°), NW (315°). Halfway between cardinal points.
key_facts: Northeast (NE) = 045°
Southeast (SE) = 135°
Southwest (SW) = 225°
Northwest (NW) = 315°
Halfway between cardinal directions (45° spacing)

syllabus_desc: Define true direction.
concept: A true direction is any bearing or track measured from true north  -  that is, from the geographic North Pole. When you measure a track on your VNC chart using a protractor, you're measuring a true direction because the chart meridians point to true north. All chart work starts in true. You then convert to magnetic (by applying variation) for actual compass steering. For example, if you measure a track of 090°T on the chart, and NZ variation is 22°E, your magnetic track would be 090° − 22° = 068°M.
exam_tip: True direction = measured from true north. What you get from chart protractor work. Convert to magnetic by applying variation.
key_facts: True direction is measured relative to true north
Chart protractor measurements give true directions
Denoted with suffix 'T' (e.g. 090°T)
Convert to magnetic by applying variation

syllabus_desc: Define magnetic direction.
concept: A magnetic direction is any bearing or track measured from magnetic north. When you apply magnetic variation to a true direction, you get a magnetic direction. In practice, your compass and directional gyro (DI) give you magnetic headings, and ATC assigns magnetic runways and headings. In NZ, where variation is about 22°E, magnetic directions are roughly 22° less than true directions (variation east, magnetic least). Magnetic direction is what you actually steer by in the cockpit.
exam_tip: Magnetic direction = measured from magnetic north. What your compass reads (before deviation). True ± variation = magnetic.
key_facts: Magnetic direction is measured from magnetic north
Obtained by applying variation to true direction
Variation East: subtract from true to get magnetic
Variation West: add to true to get magnetic
Denoted with suffix 'M' (e.g. 068°M)

syllabus_desc: Define compass direction.
concept: A compass direction is what your aircraft's magnetic compass actually reads after both variation and deviation have been accounted for. It's the final step in the conversion chain: True → Magnetic (apply variation) → Compass (apply deviation). Deviation is unique to each individual aircraft and varies with heading  -  that's why there's a deviation card mounted near the compass. For most PPL flying in NZ, compass deviation on well-maintained aircraft is small (typically ±2-3°), but you should always check the deviation card.
exam_tip: Compass direction = what the compass needle actually reads. Magnetic ± deviation = compass. Check the deviation card.
key_facts: Compass direction accounts for both variation and deviation
Conversion chain: True → Magnetic → Compass
Deviation is aircraft-specific and varies with heading
Deviation card shows corrections for each heading
Denoted with suffix 'C' (e.g. 065°C)

syllabus_desc: Define magnetic variation.
concept: Magnetic variation (or declination) is the angular difference between true north and magnetic north at a given location. In New Zealand, variation is approximately 20-23° East, meaning magnetic north is east of true north. Variation changes slowly over time and varies by location. On NZ VNC charts, isogonals (lines of equal variation) are printed as dashed magenta lines. You'll see annotations like '22°E (2020)' with an annual change rate. The old saying 'Variation East, Magnetic Least' reminds you to subtract easterly variation when converting from true to magnetic.
exam_tip: Variation = angle between true and magnetic north. NZ variation is about 20-23°E. 'Variation East, Magnetic Least'  -  subtract to convert true to magnetic.
key_facts: Variation = angular difference between true north and magnetic north
NZ variation is approximately 20-23° East
Variation changes slowly over time (secular change)
Shown on VNC charts as isogonals (dashed magenta lines)
Variation East, Magnetic Least  -  subtract for true-to-magnetic
Variation West, Magnetic Best  -  add for true-to-magnetic
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, LINZ magnetic model, NZ VNC charts

syllabus_desc: Define magnetic dip.
concept: Magnetic dip (or inclination) is the angle at which the Earth's magnetic field lines intersect the Earth's surface. At the magnetic equator, the field lines are horizontal (dip = 0°). Near the magnetic poles, they're nearly vertical (dip approaching 90°). New Zealand, being at relatively high southern latitudes, has significant magnetic dip  -  the south-seeking end of a compass needle wants to point downward. This dip is the root cause of compass turning errors and acceleration errors that you need to understand for navigation and instrument flying.
exam_tip: Magnetic dip = angle of magnetic field lines below horizontal. Zero at magnetic equator, maximum at poles. Causes compass turning and acceleration errors.
key_facts: Magnetic dip = angle of field lines below the horizontal
Dip is 0° at the magnetic equator
Dip approaches 90° near the magnetic poles
NZ has significant southerly dip
Dip causes compass turning errors and acceleration errors

syllabus_desc: Define an isogonal.
concept: An isogonal is a line on a chart joining places that have the same magnetic variation. On NZ VNC charts, isogonals appear as dashed magenta lines with the variation value annotated (e.g. '22°E 2020'). They typically run roughly north-south across the country. The line where variation is zero is called the agonic line  -  but that's nowhere near NZ. For your flight planning, read the isogonal closest to your route to determine the variation to apply. If your track crosses isogonals, use the average variation for the leg.
exam_tip: Isogonal = line joining places of equal magnetic variation. Shown as dashed magenta lines on NZ VNC charts.
key_facts: Isogonal = line of equal magnetic variation
Shown as dashed magenta lines on VNC charts
Include variation value and year of measurement
Agonic line = isogonal where variation is zero
Use the isogonal nearest your route for flight planning

syllabus_desc: Define compass deviation.
concept: Compass deviation is the error between magnetic north and compass north, caused by magnetic influences within the aircraft  -  things like the engine, electrical systems, metal structure, and avionics. Unlike variation (which depends on your position on Earth), deviation depends on the individual aircraft and changes with heading. Every aircraft has a compass deviation card, usually mounted near the compass, showing the correction for various headings. A well-maintained compass in a properly swung aircraft will typically have deviation of no more than ±2-3°.
exam_tip: Deviation = compass error caused by magnetic fields in the aircraft. Aircraft-specific, changes with heading. Check the deviation card.
key_facts: Deviation = difference between magnetic north and compass north
Caused by aircraft magnetic fields (engine, electrics, structure)
Varies with aircraft heading
Each aircraft has a deviation card showing corrections
Compass swing procedure calibrates and records deviation

syllabus_desc: Define true bearing.
concept: A true bearing is the direction of one point from another, measured clockwise from true north. When you use a protractor on a chart to measure the direction from your position to a landmark, you're measuring a true bearing. For example, if Tauranga aerodrome bears 090° from your position measured from true north, that's a bearing of 090°T. True bearings are what you work with on the chart before converting to magnetic for actual cockpit use.
exam_tip: True bearing = direction from one point to another measured clockwise from true north. What you measure on a chart.
key_facts: True bearing measured clockwise from true north
Measured using a protractor on a chart
Suffix 'T' indicates true bearing (e.g. 090°T)
Must be converted to magnetic for cockpit use

syllabus_desc: Define magnetic bearing.
concept: A magnetic bearing is the direction of one point from another, measured clockwise from magnetic north. You get it by applying magnetic variation to a true bearing. Since NZ variation is approximately 22°E, a true bearing of 090°T becomes a magnetic bearing of 068°M (090 − 22 = 068). Magnetic bearings are what you'd read from your compass or DI when pointing toward a landmark. ATC directions and runway headings in NZ are given in magnetic.
exam_tip: Magnetic bearing = direction measured from magnetic north. True bearing ± variation = magnetic bearing. ATC uses magnetic.
key_facts: Magnetic bearing measured clockwise from magnetic north
True bearing ± variation = magnetic bearing
Suffix 'M' indicates magnetic (e.g. 068°M)
ATC headings and runway designations are magnetic

syllabus_desc: Define compass bearing.
concept: A compass bearing is the direction of one point from another as actually indicated by the aircraft's magnetic compass. It includes the effects of both magnetic variation and compass deviation. You get it by applying deviation to the magnetic bearing. In practice, compass bearings are mainly relevant when using the compass directly (rather than the DI). For most PPL navigation, the difference between magnetic and compass bearings is small if the compass has been properly swung.
exam_tip: Compass bearing = magnetic bearing ± deviation. What the aircraft compass actually shows.
key_facts: Compass bearing includes both variation and deviation
Magnetic bearing ± deviation = compass bearing
Suffix 'C' indicates compass bearing
Mainly relevant when reading the magnetic compass directly

syllabus_desc: Define relative bearing.
concept: A relative bearing is the direction of an object measured clockwise from the aircraft's nose (heading). So something directly ahead is at a relative bearing of 000°, something off the right wing is 090°, directly behind is 180°, and off the left wing is 270°. Relative bearings are useful for quickly communicating the position of traffic, landmarks, or other points of interest relative to your aircraft. To convert a relative bearing to a true bearing, add it to your true heading.
exam_tip: Relative bearing = direction from the aircraft's nose, measured clockwise. Ahead = 000°, right = 090°, behind = 180°, left = 270°.
key_facts: Relative bearing measured clockwise from the aircraft's nose (heading)
0° = directly ahead, 090° = right, 180° = behind, 270° = left
True bearing = true heading + relative bearing
Used for traffic and landmark callouts

syllabus_desc: Define relative bearing using the clock method.
concept: The clock method is a quick way to describe a relative bearing using clock positions. The aircraft's nose is 12 o'clock, the right wing is 3 o'clock, the tail is 6 o'clock, and the left wing is 9 o'clock. So 'traffic at your 2 o'clock' means roughly 60° to the right of your nose. Each hour represents 30° of arc. It's not as precise as a three-digit bearing, but it's fast and intuitive  -  especially useful for traffic calls from ATC or other pilots. 'Traffic 10 o'clock, 5 miles' is immediately understandable.
exam_tip: Clock method: 12 = ahead, 3 = right, 6 = behind, 9 = left. Each hour = 30°. Quick and intuitive for traffic calls.
key_facts: 12 o'clock = directly ahead (000° relative)
3 o'clock = right wing (090° relative)
6 o'clock = behind (180° relative)
9 o'clock = left wing (270° relative)
Each clock hour = 30° of relative bearing

syllabus_desc: Convert between true, magnetic and compass directions.
concept: The conversion chain is True → Magnetic → Compass. Going from true to magnetic, apply variation: for easterly variation (like NZ's ~22°E), subtract. Remember 'Variation East, Magnetic Least.' Going from magnetic to compass, apply deviation from the aircraft's deviation card. To go the other way (compass → magnetic → true), reverse the process. Here's a worked example: True track 110°T, variation 22°E, deviation 2°W. Magnetic = 110 − 22 = 088°M. Compass = 088 + 2 = 090°C. Get comfortable with this  -  you'll use it on every cross-country flight.
exam_tip: True ± variation = Magnetic ± deviation = Compass. 'Variation East, Magnetic Least.' Practise the conversion chain both ways.
key_facts: True → Magnetic: apply variation (East subtract, West add)
Magnetic → Compass: apply deviation (from deviation card)
Mnemonic: 'Variation East, Magnetic Least; Variation West, Magnetic Best'
Reverse the process to convert back (Compass → Magnetic → True)
NZ variation ~22°E  -  so magnetic is about 22° less than true

syllabus_desc: Convert between relative, true, magnetic and compass bearings.
concept: To convert a relative bearing to a true bearing: True Bearing = True Heading + Relative Bearing. If the result is greater than 360°, subtract 360°. For example, if your true heading is 350°T and you see a landmark at a relative bearing of 040°, the true bearing to it is 350 + 40 = 390 − 360 = 030°T. From there, you can convert to magnetic by applying variation. The clock method converts similarly  -  just multiply clock hours by 30° to get the approximate relative bearing first.
exam_tip: True bearing = true heading + relative bearing (subtract 360° if over). Then apply variation for magnetic.
key_facts: True bearing = true heading + relative bearing
If result > 360°, subtract 360°
Then apply variation to get magnetic bearing
Clock position × 30° gives approximate relative bearing
Works in reverse to find relative bearing from true bearing

syllabus_desc: Explain the processes, cautions and limitations when deriving track distances and bearings from a chart.
concept: When measuring tracks and distances on a chart, you need to be aware of several things. Place your protractor on the meridian nearest the mid-point of the track for the most accurate bearing (because convergence of meridians means the track angle changes along its length). For distance, use the latitude scale on the side of the chart  -  one minute of latitude equals one nautical mile. Don't use the longitude scale for distance, because the spacing of longitude lines changes with latitude. On NZ VNC charts, also watch for chart joins and be careful not to misread elevation contours as track lines.
exam_tip: Measure bearings at the mid-meridian of your track. Use the latitude scale for distance (1 min lat = 1 nm). Don't use the longitude scale for distance.
key_facts: Measure track angles at the meridian nearest the mid-point of the leg
Meridian convergence means track angle varies along a long leg
Use the latitude scale for distance: 1 minute of latitude = 1 nm
Do NOT use the longitude scale for distance measurement
Be aware of chart joins and edge distortions on VNC charts
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, NZ VNC chart usage

syllabus_desc: Define statute mile.
concept: A statute mile is a land-based unit of distance equal to 5,280 feet (1,609 metres or 1.609 km). It's the 'normal' mile used in everyday life and on road signs. In aviation, we generally don't use statute miles for distance  -  we use nautical miles instead. However, you might encounter statute miles in visibility reports from some countries (not NZ  -  we use metres and kilometres). The key thing to know is how to convert: 1 statute mile = 0.869 nautical miles, or roughly, a statute mile is shorter than a nautical mile.
exam_tip: Statute mile = 5,280 feet (1,609 m). The 'land mile.' Not used for aviation distance in NZ  -  we use nautical miles.
key_facts: 1 statute mile = 5,280 feet = 1,609 metres
The everyday 'land' mile
Shorter than a nautical mile
Not commonly used in NZ aviation
1 statute mile ≈ 0.869 nautical miles

syllabus_desc: Define nautical mile.
concept: The nautical mile is the standard unit of distance in aviation and at sea. One nautical mile (nm) equals 1,852 metres and corresponds to one minute of latitude on the Earth's surface. This is what makes it so useful  -  you can measure distance directly from the latitude scale on your chart. The nautical mile is slightly longer than a statute mile (1 nm = 1.15 statute miles). All distances in NZ aviation  -  flight plans, chart measurements, ATIS reports, ATC clearances  -  are in nautical miles.
exam_tip: 1 nautical mile = 1,852 m = 1 minute of latitude. Standard aviation distance unit. Measure it directly from the chart's latitude scale.
key_facts: 1 nautical mile = 1,852 metres
Equals 1 minute of latitude on the Earth's surface
Standard unit of distance in aviation
1 nm = 1.15 statute miles = 1.852 km
Can be measured directly from the latitude scale on a chart

syllabus_desc: Define kilometre.
concept: A kilometre (km) is 1,000 metres. It's the standard metric unit of distance used in most countries for land measurement. In NZ aviation, kilometres are used for visibility distances (e.g. 'visibility 10 km' in METARs and TAFs) and for some altitude/height expressions in certain contexts. For navigation distances though, we stick to nautical miles. The conversion is: 1 nm = 1.852 km, so a nautical mile is almost exactly 1.85 kilometres.
exam_tip: 1 kilometre = 1,000 metres. Used for visibility in aviation. Navigation distances use nautical miles. 1 nm = 1.852 km.
key_facts: 1 kilometre = 1,000 metres
Used for visibility reporting in METARs/TAFs
Not used for navigation distance in NZ aviation

syllabus_desc: Define metre.
concept: A metre is the SI base unit of length. In NZ aviation, metres are used for runway dimensions (e.g. Ardmore runway 03L/21R is 1,219 metres long), aerodrome elevation, obstacle clearance heights, and visibility when less than 5 km. Cloud base in METARs is expressed in feet, not metres. The key conversions: 1 metre = 3.281 feet, and 1 foot = 0.3048 metres. You'll need to be able to convert between these for flight planning.
exam_tip: 1 metre = 3.281 feet. Used for runway lengths, aerodrome elevation, and visibility. Cloud and altitude use feet.
key_facts: SI base unit of length
1 metre = 3.281 feet
Used for runway lengths and aerodrome elevation
Used for visibility in METARs/TAFs
Altitude and cloud base expressed in feet, not metres

syllabus_desc: Define foot.
concept: A foot (ft) is the primary unit for altitude and height in aviation. One foot equals 0.3048 metres. All altitude references  -  circuit height, cruise level, transition altitude, cloud base  -  are expressed in feet. An altimeter reads in feet. NZ cruising levels are expressed in feet AMSL (e.g. 'maintain 4,500 feet'). The conversion to know: multiply feet by 0.3048 to get metres, or roughly divide by 3.3. So 3,000 feet is about 914 metres.
exam_tip: 1 foot = 0.3048 metres. The standard unit for all altitude and height in aviation.
key_facts: 1 foot = 0.3048 metres (or roughly divide by 3.3)
Standard unit for altitude, height, and elevation in aviation
Altimeters read in feet
Cloud base, cruising levels, and circuit heights all in feet

syllabus_desc: Calculate the conversion between a statute mile, a nautical mile and a kilometre.
concept: Here are the key conversions you need to have cold: 1 nautical mile = 1.852 km = 1.15 statute miles. Or turning it around: 1 statute mile = 1.609 km = 0.869 nm, and 1 km = 0.54 nm = 0.621 statute miles. The most common conversion you'll do in NZ is between nautical miles and kilometres. A quick mental trick: to convert nm to km, double it and subtract about 15%. So 100 nm ≈ 185 km. For the exam, know the exact figures.
exam_tip: 1 nm = 1.852 km = 1.15 SM. 1 SM = 1.609 km = 0.869 nm. Quick trick: nm × 1.852 = km.
key_facts: 1 nautical mile = 1.852 km = 1.15 statute miles
1 statute mile = 1.609 km = 0.869 nm
1 kilometre = 0.54 nm = 0.621 statute miles
Quick check: nm × 1.85 ≈ km
These conversions will appear in exam questions

syllabus_desc: Measure distances up to 300nm (± 1%) on an appropriate chart.
concept: To measure distance on a NZ VNC chart, use the latitude scale on the side of the chart  -  remember, one minute of latitude equals one nautical mile. Use a pair of dividers or a ruler. For short distances, span the dividers between two points and read off the latitude scale. For longer distances, set your dividers to a convenient span (say 10 nm or 20 nm) and 'walk' them along the track, counting as you go. Always use the latitude scale at roughly the same latitude as your measurement  -  the scale can vary slightly from top to bottom of the chart.
exam_tip: Use the latitude scale on VNC charts (1 min lat = 1 nm). For long distances, 'walk' dividers along the track. Use the scale at the same latitude as your route.
key_facts: 1 minute of latitude = 1 nautical mile
Use the latitude scale on the side of the chart
For short legs: span dividers between points
For long legs: 'walk' dividers along the track in set increments
Use the latitude scale at the same latitude as your measurement
Accuracy target: ± 1% for distances up to 300 nm

syllabus_desc: Define a knot (kt).
concept: A knot is one nautical mile per hour. It's the standard unit of speed in aviation and maritime use. So if you're flying at 100 knots, you're covering 100 nautical miles every hour. Wind speeds are given in knots, your airspeed indicator reads in knots, and ATC assigns speeds in knots. Don't say 'knots per hour'  -  that's like saying 'nautical miles per hour per hour,' which makes no sense. Just 'knots.' The abbreviation is 'kt' (singular and plural).
exam_tip: 1 knot = 1 nautical mile per hour. Standard aviation speed unit. Don't say 'knots per hour'  -  just 'knots.'
key_facts: 1 knot (kt) = 1 nautical mile per hour
Standard unit of speed in aviation
Wind speeds, airspeeds, and ground speeds all in knots
Never say 'knots per hour' (it's already 'per hour')

syllabus_desc: Define ground speed (GS).
concept: Ground speed is your actual speed over the ground  -  how fast you're moving relative to the Earth's surface. It's what determines how long a leg will take. Ground speed is NOT shown on your airspeed indicator; it's the result of your true airspeed plus or minus the wind effect. A headwind reduces your GS below your TAS; a tailwind increases it. On a day with no wind, your GS equals your TAS. You calculate GS during flight planning using the triangle of velocities, and you can check it in flight by timing between known ground features.
exam_tip: Ground speed = speed over the ground. TAS adjusted for wind. Determines your actual travel time on each leg.
key_facts: Ground speed (GS) = actual speed over the Earth's surface
Not shown on the airspeed indicator
GS = TAS ± wind component along track
Headwind reduces GS; tailwind increases GS
Determines leg times and ETAs

syllabus_desc: Define indicated airspeed (IAS).
concept: Indicated airspeed (IAS) is the speed shown on your airspeed indicator (ASI). It's derived from the difference between pitot pressure (ram air) and static pressure. IAS is what you use for flying the aircraft  -  your circuit speeds, approach speeds, and Vne are all IAS values. However, IAS has errors: instrument error and position error (caused by the pitot/static system location on the aircraft). When you correct IAS for these errors, you get calibrated airspeed (CAS). At sea level in standard conditions, IAS is close to TAS.
exam_tip: IAS = speed shown on the ASI. Used for all aircraft handling speeds. Contains instrument and position errors.
key_facts: IAS = reading on the airspeed indicator (ASI)
Derived from pitot pressure minus static pressure
Used for aircraft handling (Vs, Va, Vne, circuit speeds)
Contains instrument error and position error
IAS ≈ TAS at sea level in standard conditions

syllabus_desc: Define calibrated airspeed (CAS).
concept: Calibrated airspeed is IAS corrected for instrument error and position error. Position error comes from the location of the pitot tube and static ports on the aircraft  -  at certain flight attitudes, the airflow over these sensors isn't perfectly aligned, introducing small errors. The Aircraft Flight Manual (AFM) or Pilot Operating Handbook (POH) usually includes a table or graph showing the correction from IAS to CAS. For most light aircraft at normal cruise attitudes, the difference between IAS and CAS is small  -  often just 1-3 knots.
exam_tip: CAS = IAS corrected for instrument and position error. Check the AFM for the correction table. Usually a small correction.
key_facts: CAS = IAS corrected for instrument and position errors
Position error depends on pitot/static port location and attitude
AFM provides IAS-to-CAS correction data
Difference is typically small (1-3 kt) in normal flight
CAS is the input for calculating TAS

syllabus_desc: Define true airspeed (TAS).
concept: True airspeed is your actual speed through the air mass  -  CAS corrected for air density (altitude and temperature). As you climb, the air gets thinner, so the same CAS results in a higher TAS. The rule of thumb is that TAS increases by about 2% per 1,000 feet of altitude. You calculate TAS using your navigation computer (E6B or electronic) by entering CAS, pressure altitude, and outside air temperature. TAS is what you use for flight planning calculations  -  it goes into the triangle of velocities along with wind to give you heading and ground speed.
exam_tip: TAS = CAS corrected for altitude and temperature (density). Increases ~2% per 1,000 ft. Used for flight planning calculations.
key_facts: TAS = CAS corrected for air density (altitude and temperature)
TAS increases approximately 2% per 1,000 feet
Calculated using navigation computer (CAS + pressure altitude + OAT)
TAS is used for flight planning and the triangle of velocities
In still air, TAS = ground speed

syllabus_desc: Explain the difference between speed and velocity.
concept: Speed is a scalar quantity  -  it tells you how fast something is moving, but not in which direction. Velocity is a vector quantity  -  it has both magnitude (speed) and direction. In navigation, this matters because wind has a velocity (speed AND direction), and your aircraft has a velocity through the air (TAS in a specific heading direction). Ground speed alone is a scalar  -  but ground velocity is your speed and track direction combined. The triangle of velocities is all about combining vector quantities to solve navigation problems.
exam_tip: Speed = how fast (magnitude only). Velocity = how fast AND in which direction (magnitude + direction). Navigation uses velocities (vectors).
key_facts: Speed is scalar (magnitude only, no direction)
Velocity is a vector (magnitude AND direction)
Wind velocity = wind speed + direction it's coming from
TAS + heading = air velocity vector
GS + track = ground velocity vector

syllabus_desc: Define ground position.
concept: Your ground position is the point on the Earth's surface directly below the aircraft. It's where you actually are, right now, as confirmed by visual reference to known features below you. If you can look down and say 'I'm directly over the Hamilton control tower,' that's your ground position. It's the most definitive type of position because it's based on positive identification of a known feature.
exam_tip: Ground position = the point on the Earth's surface directly below the aircraft. Based on positive identification of features below.
key_facts: Point on the Earth's surface directly below the aircraft
Determined by visual identification of ground features
Most definitive type of position
Confirmed by looking down and identifying known features

syllabus_desc: Define DR position.
concept: A DR (deduced reckoning, often called 'dead reckoning') position is your estimated position based on calculations  -  your last known position, heading, airspeed, wind, and elapsed time. It's where you think you should be, not necessarily where you actually are. DR positions degrade in accuracy over time because they accumulate errors from wind changes, heading drift, and imprecise timing. That's why you always want to confirm your DR position with visual fixes whenever possible. The longer since your last fix, the less reliable your DR position.
exam_tip: DR position = estimated position based on calculations from last known position, heading, speed, and time. Gets less accurate the longer since your last fix.
key_facts: DR = deduced (dead) reckoning position
Calculated from last known position + heading + speed + time
An estimate, not a confirmed position
Accuracy degrades over time as errors accumulate
Should be confirmed with visual fixes regularly

syllabus_desc: Define fix.
concept: A fix is a confirmed position determined by direct observation  -  visual identification of a ground feature, intersection of two or more position lines, or from a navigation aid. A fix is far more reliable than a DR position because it's based on actual observation rather than calculation. In VFR navigation over NZ, your fixes come from identifying ground features: towns, lakes, rivers, coastline, prominent hills, airfields. Every time you positively identify your position, that's a fix, and it resets the accuracy of your navigation for the next leg.
exam_tip: A fix = confirmed position from actual observation (visual features, nav aids, or intersecting position lines). More reliable than a DR position.
key_facts: Fix = confirmed position from direct observation
More reliable than a DR position
VFR fixes come from identifying ground features
Resets navigation accuracy for the next leg
Can also come from intersecting position lines

syllabus_desc: Define position line.
concept: A position line is a line on which you know you're located, but not exactly where on that line. For example, if you see a railway line passing below you, you know you're somewhere on that railway  -  that's a position line. If you can identify a bearing from a known feature, you're somewhere along that bearing line. To get a fix, you need at least two position lines that intersect  -  the intersection point is your fix. In VFR navigation, common position lines include bearings from landmarks, railways, rivers, roads, coastlines, and power lines.
exam_tip: Position line = a line on which you know you're located. Need two intersecting position lines to get a fix.
key_facts: A line on which the aircraft is known to be located
One position line gives a line, not a point
Two intersecting position lines give a fix
Examples: bearing from a landmark, railway, river, road, coastline
More position lines = higher confidence in the fix

syllabus_desc: Describe and apply position referencing by place name.
concept: The simplest way to describe your position is by reference to a place name  -  'overhead Taupo,' 'abeam Hamilton,' or 'approaching Paraparaumu.' This works when you're close to or at a well-known feature. In NZ aviation, common reference points include towns, aerodromes, prominent geographic features (Lake Taupo, Mount Ruapehu, Cook Strait), and reporting points published in AIPNZ. Place name referencing is quick and unambiguous when used for well-known locations.
exam_tip: Position by place name: 'overhead Taupo,' 'abeam Hamilton.' Works best for well-known features near your position.
key_facts: Simplest method: reference a well-known place
'Overhead,' 'abeam,' 'approaching,' 'departed'
Use published reporting points from AIPNZ
Common NZ references: towns, aerodromes, lakes, mountains
Quick and unambiguous for well-known locations

syllabus_desc: Describe and apply position referencing by place/bearing/distance.
concept: When you're not directly over a known feature, describe your position by giving a place name, a bearing FROM that place, and a distance. For example: '15 nm south of Tauranga' or 'Ardmore 090 radial, 25 nm.' The bearing is always FROM the reference point, measured magnetic. This method gives a precise position when a simple place name isn't enough. It's how NZ pilots commonly report position to ATC when not at a published reporting point.
exam_tip: Position by place/bearing/distance: '15 nm south of Tauranga.' Bearing is always FROM the reference point, measured magnetic.
key_facts: Three components: place name + magnetic bearing FROM it + distance
Example: 'Hamilton 090M, 20 nm' = 20 nm east of Hamilton
Bearing is always FROM the reference point
Bearing given in magnetic
Common NZ position reporting method

syllabus_desc: Describe and apply position referencing by latitude and longitude.
concept: Latitude and longitude coordinates give an exact position anywhere on Earth. Expressed in degrees and minutes (e.g. 36°50'S 174°46'E for Auckland Airport). This method is precise but less intuitive for quick communications. It's used in flight plans, search and rescue operations, and when describing positions in remote areas where there are no convenient landmarks. GPS units display position in lat/long. Remember: latitude first (N or S), then longitude (E or W).
exam_tip: Lat/long gives exact position: e.g. 36°50'S 174°46'E. Used in flight plans, SAR, and remote areas. Latitude first, then longitude.
key_facts: Format: degrees and minutes, latitude then longitude
Example: 36°50'S 174°46'E (Auckland Airport)
Precise and unambiguous position description
Used in flight plans, SAR operations, and remote areas
GPS units display position in lat/long format

syllabus_desc: Describe and apply position referencing using relative bearing and the clock face method.
concept: To describe the position of another aircraft or an object relative to you, use the clock face method: '2 o'clock, 5 miles, same level.' This tells you (or another pilot) the direction to look (2 o'clock = 60° right of your nose), the distance, and the altitude relationship. ATC commonly uses this format for traffic information. Remember, the clock position is relative to your heading  -  if you change heading, the clock position of a fixed object changes. Each clock hour equals 30° of relative bearing.
exam_tip: Clock face method for relative position: '2 o'clock, 5 miles, same level.' Relative to your heading. Each hour = 30°.
key_facts: Describes position of other traffic relative to your aircraft
Format: clock position + distance + altitude relationship
12 o'clock = ahead, 3 = right, 6 = behind, 9 = left
Each clock hour = 30° of relative bearing
Clock position changes if you change heading

syllabus_desc: Define height.
concept: Height is the vertical distance of an aircraft above a specific ground reference point  -  typically the aerodrome surface. When your instructor says 'circuit height is 1,000 feet,' they mean 1,000 feet above the aerodrome elevation. Height is measured above ground level (AGL). It's relevant for things like circuit flying, obstacle clearance near an aerodrome, and terrain avoidance. The key distinction: height is always above a ground reference, while altitude is above mean sea level.
exam_tip: Height = vertical distance above a ground reference point (AGL). Circuit height is above the aerodrome. Not the same as altitude.
key_facts: Height = vertical distance above a ground reference point
Measured above ground level (AGL)
Used for circuit height, obstacle clearance near aerodromes
Example: circuit height 1,000 ft AGL
Different from altitude (which is above MSL)

syllabus_desc: Define altitude.
concept: Altitude is the vertical distance of an aircraft above mean sea level (MSL). When your altimeter is set to the local QNH, it reads altitude. All NZ cruising levels, airspace boundaries, and most ATC altitude assignments are expressed as altitude AMSL. For example, 'maintain 4,500 feet' means 4,500 feet above mean sea level. Altitude is the common reference that allows all aircraft and ATC to work from the same baseline, regardless of the terrain height below.
exam_tip: Altitude = vertical distance above mean sea level (MSL). What your altimeter reads when set to QNH. The standard reference for cruising and ATC.
key_facts: Altitude = vertical distance above mean sea level (MSL)
Altimeter reads altitude when set to QNH
Standard reference for cruising levels and ATC
Expressed as feet AMSL
Common baseline regardless of terrain below

syllabus_desc: Define mean sea level (MSL).
concept: Mean sea level is the average level of the sea surface, calculated over a long period to smooth out tides and weather effects. It serves as the zero reference (datum) for measuring altitude in aviation. All spot heights on charts, aerodrome elevations, terrain contours, and obstacle heights are referenced to MSL. In New Zealand, the MSL datum used is based on tide gauges around the NZ coast. When you see '4,500 ft' on a chart or in an ATC instruction, that's 4,500 feet above MSL.
exam_tip: MSL = the average sea level, used as the zero datum for all altitude measurements in aviation.
key_facts: MSL = average sea surface level over time
Zero reference for altitude measurement in aviation
Chart spot heights, elevations, and obstacles reference MSL
NZ MSL datum based on NZ coastal tide gauges
AMSL = above mean sea level

syllabus_desc: Define AMSL.
concept: AMSL stands for 'Above Mean Sea Level.' It's the standard way of expressing altitude in NZ aviation. When the ATIS says 'QNH 1018, altitude readout 350 feet,' or your chart shows 'Ardmore elevation 111 ft,' those are AMSL values. All published aerodrome elevations, terrain heights, obstacle heights, and airspace boundaries in NZ are expressed in feet AMSL. It's the universal vertical reference that keeps everyone on the same page.
exam_tip: AMSL = Above Mean Sea Level. The standard expression for altitude in NZ. Aerodrome elevations, terrain, obstacles, and airspace are all AMSL.
key_facts: AMSL = Above Mean Sea Level
Standard altitude reference in NZ aviation
Aerodrome elevations expressed in feet AMSL
Chart terrain and obstacle heights are AMSL
Airspace boundaries defined in feet AMSL

syllabus_desc: Define ground level.
concept: Ground level is the actual elevation of the terrain surface at a particular point. It varies constantly  -  from sea level on the coast to over 12,000 feet at the top of Aoraki/Mount Cook. Ground level is what you need to think about for terrain clearance. Your altitude (AMSL) minus the ground level at that point gives you your height above ground. If you're cruising at 4,500 ft and the terrain below is at 2,000 ft, you have 2,500 ft of terrain clearance.
exam_tip: Ground level = actual terrain elevation at a point. Your altitude minus ground level = your height above ground (terrain clearance).
key_facts: Ground level = elevation of the terrain surface
Varies across the landscape
Altitude (AMSL) − ground level = height AGL
Critical for terrain clearance planning
NZ terrain ranges from sea level to over 12,000 ft (Aoraki/Mount Cook)

syllabus_desc: Define AGL.
concept: AGL stands for 'Above Ground Level.' It expresses height relative to the terrain directly below the aircraft. Circuit heights are given AGL (e.g. 1,000 ft AGL means 1,000 feet above the aerodrome surface). Some airspace boundaries are expressed AGL too  -  for example, Class G airspace might extend from the surface to a specified height AGL. The challenge with AGL is that the ground level changes, so your AGL height varies as terrain changes beneath you, even if your altimeter reading (altitude AMSL) stays constant.
exam_tip: AGL = Above Ground Level. Used for circuit heights and some airspace. Changes as terrain changes below you.
key_facts: AGL = Above Ground Level
Height above the terrain directly below
Used for circuit heights (e.g. 1,000 ft AGL)
Some airspace boundaries defined in AGL
AGL height changes as terrain elevation changes below

syllabus_desc: Define elevation.
concept: Elevation is the vertical distance of a fixed point on the Earth's surface above mean sea level. Aerodrome elevation is the highest point on the usable runway surfaces. For example, Ardmore's elevation is 111 ft AMSL, Queenstown is 1,171 ft AMSL. Elevation is a fixed, published value  -  unlike altitude (which changes as you fly) or height (which is relative to the ground). You need aerodrome elevation to calculate your circuit altitude: circuit altitude = aerodrome elevation + circuit height (AGL).
exam_tip: Elevation = height of a fixed ground point above MSL. Aerodrome elevation = highest point on usable runways. Used to calculate circuit altitude.
key_facts: Elevation = vertical distance of a ground point above MSL
A fixed, published value
Aerodrome elevation = highest point on usable runway surfaces
Circuit altitude = aerodrome elevation + circuit height AGL
Published in AIPNZ Vol 4 for each aerodrome
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, AIPNZ Vol 4

syllabus_desc: Define pressure altitude (PA).
concept: Pressure altitude is what your altimeter reads when it's set to the standard pressure datum of 1013.25 hPa (also called QNE). It's the altitude in the International Standard Atmosphere (ISA) that corresponds to the ambient pressure around the aircraft. Pressure altitude doesn't account for local atmospheric conditions  -  that's why it won't match your actual altitude above sea level unless the QNH happens to be exactly 1013.25 hPa. You use pressure altitude for TAS calculations on your navigation computer and for performance charts in the AFM.
exam_tip: Pressure altitude = altimeter reading with 1013.25 hPa set. Used for TAS calculations and performance charts. Not the same as actual altitude unless QNH = 1013.
key_facts: Pressure altitude = altimeter reading at standard setting (1013.25 hPa / QNE)
Based on the International Standard Atmosphere (ISA)
Used for TAS calculations on the navigation computer
Used for performance charts in the AFM
Does not reflect actual altitude above MSL unless QNH = 1013.25

syllabus_desc: Define QNH.
concept: QNH is the atmospheric pressure at mean sea level at a particular location. When you set QNH on your altimeter subscale, the altimeter reads your altitude above mean sea level (AMSL). QNH is provided by ATIS broadcasts, AWIS (Automated Weather Information Service), and ATC in NZ. It changes with weather systems  -  high pressure systems give higher QNH values, low pressure gives lower. You must always ensure your altimeter is set to the current local or area QNH when flying in NZ. 'QNH one-zero-one-eight' means set 1018 hPa on your subscale.
exam_tip: QNH = atmospheric pressure at MSL. Set QNH on your altimeter to read altitude AMSL. Obtained from ATIS, AWIS, or ATC.
key_facts: QNH = atmospheric pressure adjusted to mean sea level
Setting QNH gives altitude above MSL on the altimeter
Obtained from ATIS, AWIS, MetFlight, or ATC
Changes with weather systems (high pressure = high QNH)
Must be kept current during flight
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, AIPNZ ENR 1.7

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of a change in mean sea level air pressure on the altimeter reading of a transiting aircraft.
concept: Here's the classic trap: if you fly from an area of high pressure to an area of low pressure without updating your QNH, your altimeter will over-read  -  it'll show you higher than you actually are. The old saying 'high to low, look out below' captures this perfectly. The altimeter thinks the pressure at sea level is still the higher value, so it underestimates how far the pressure has dropped, and therefore overestimates your altitude. The reverse is true too  -  low to high means your altimeter reads lower than actual. This is why you must keep your QNH updated as you transit through different pressure areas.
exam_tip: 'High to low, look out below.' Flying into lower pressure without updating QNH = altimeter over-reads = you're lower than you think. Always update QNH.
key_facts: High to low pressure without QNH update: altimeter over-reads (you're lower than shown)
Low to high pressure without QNH update: altimeter under-reads (you're higher than shown)
Mnemonic: 'High to low, look out below'
Must update QNH when transiting between pressure areas
Each 1 hPa of QNH error ≈ 30 ft of altitude error
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, AIPNZ ENR 1.7

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of a change in temperature on the altimeter reading of a transiting aircraft.
concept: Temperature also affects your altimeter, and it works similarly to pressure. If you fly from warm air into cold air without any other changes, your altimeter will over-read  -  showing you higher than you actually are. Cold air is denser, so pressure levels are pushed closer to the ground. The saying extends: 'from hot to cold, don't be bold' or 'cold and low, look out below.' This is particularly relevant in NZ when flying from warmer North Island conditions toward the cooler Southern Alps. Temperature corrections become important for terrain clearance in cold conditions.
exam_tip: 'From hot to cold, don't be bold.' Cold air makes your altimeter over-read  -  you're lower than indicated. Important for NZ mountain flying.
key_facts: Flying into colder air: altimeter over-reads (you're lower than shown)
Flying into warmer air: altimeter under-reads (you're higher than shown)
Cold air is denser  -  pressure levels are lower than standard
Mnemonic: 'From hot to cold, don't be bold'
Especially important for mountain flying in the Southern Alps

syllabus_desc: State and apply the altimeter setting rules in New Zealand.
concept: In NZ, the rules are straightforward: below the transition level, set the area or local QNH on your altimeter so it reads altitude AMSL. QNH is obtained from ATIS, AWIS, MetFlight, or ATC. For VFR flight, you generally keep the QNH set throughout. When operating near an aerodrome, use the local QNH if available. When transiting between areas, update to the relevant area QNH. The area QNH sectors are published in AIPNZ ENR 1.7. If you can't obtain QNH, use the last known QNH. CAR Part 91 requires your altimeter to be set to an appropriate QNH at all times during flight.
exam_tip: VFR in NZ: set area or local QNH at all times. Update QNH when transiting between areas. See AIPNZ ENR 1.7 for area QNH sectors.
key_facts: Set local or area QNH below transition level
QNH from ATIS, AWIS, MetFlight, or ATC
Update QNH when transiting between QNH areas
NZ area QNH sectors published in AIPNZ ENR 1.7
CAR Part 91 requires appropriate QNH setting at all times
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, CAR Part 91, AIPNZ ENR 1.7

syllabus_desc: Explain and apply the table of cruising levels.
concept: NZ VFR cruising levels are based on your magnetic track direction. For tracks from 360° through to 179° magnetic (roughly heading eastish), cruise at odd thousands plus 500: 3,500, 5,500, 7,500, etc. For tracks from 180° through to 359° magnetic (roughly heading westish), cruise at even thousands plus 500: 4,500, 6,500, 8,500, etc. These rules apply above 3,000 ft AGL. The '+500' part is the VFR offset  -  IFR traffic cruises at the even/odd thousands, so VFR is 500 feet above to provide separation. This is the semi-circular rule applied to NZ's magnetic environment.
exam_tip: VFR cruising levels above 3,000 ft AGL: Track 360-179°M = odd thousands + 500 (3,500, 5,500...). Track 180-359°M = even thousands + 500 (4,500, 6,500...).
key_facts: Based on magnetic track direction
Applies above 3,000 ft AGL
VFR is 500 ft above IFR levels for separation
Based on CAR Part 91.311
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, CAR Part 91.311, AIPNZ ENR 1.7

syllabus_desc: Define true and magnetic track required.
concept: Track required (TR) is the direction you want to travel over the ground from one point to another. The true track required is measured from your chart  -  it's the angle between true north and the line you want to fly. The magnetic track required is the true track corrected for variation. On your nav log, you'll measure the true track from the chart, then subtract NZ's easterly variation (about 22°) to get the magnetic track. The track required is not the same as your heading  -  wind will push you off track, so you need to crab into the wind to maintain the desired track.
exam_tip: Track required = desired direction over the ground. True track from chart, apply variation to get magnetic track. Not the same as heading.
key_facts: Track required = desired path over the ground
True track measured from the chart using a protractor
Magnetic track = true track ± variation
Track ≠ heading (heading must account for wind drift)
Abbreviated as TR (track required)

syllabus_desc: Define wind velocity (W/V).
concept: Wind velocity is the combination of wind direction and wind speed. It's expressed as a direction the wind is blowing FROM, in degrees true, and a speed in knots. For example, W/V 270/25 means wind from 270° true at 25 knots (a westerly at 25 kt). Winds aloft forecasts and area forecasts give wind velocities for different altitudes. These are critical for flight planning  -  they determine your drift, heading correction, and ground speed. Note that surface wind (from ATIS/METAR) is given in degrees magnetic, but winds aloft are given in degrees true.
exam_tip: Wind velocity = direction FROM + speed. Example: 270/25 = from the west at 25 kt. Winds aloft are in degrees TRUE. Surface wind is MAGNETIC.
key_facts: W/V = wind direction and speed combined
Direction is where wind blows FROM
Format: direction/speed (e.g. 270/25)
Winds aloft forecasts: direction in degrees TRUE
Surface winds (METAR/ATIS): direction in degrees MAGNETIC
Essential for calculating drift, heading, and ground speed

syllabus_desc: Define head/tail wind.
concept: A headwind is wind blowing from a direction that opposes your direction of travel  -  it slows you down, reducing your ground speed below your TAS. A tailwind blows from behind, boosting your ground speed above your TAS. A direct headwind or tailwind is when the wind is exactly aligned with your track. In practice, wind is rarely perfectly aligned, so you get a headwind or tailwind component (the portion of the wind along your track) plus a crosswind component (the portion across your track). Headwinds increase flight time; tailwinds decrease it.
exam_tip: Headwind = into you, slows ground speed. Tailwind = behind you, increases ground speed. Rarely exactly aligned  -  usually a component.
key_facts: Headwind blows against your direction of travel
Reduces ground speed below TAS
Tailwind blows with your direction of travel
Increases ground speed above TAS
Usually a component (HW/TW) rather than exactly aligned

syllabus_desc: Define cross wind.
concept: A crosswind is the component of wind blowing across your direction of travel (or across the runway). It pushes you sideways  -  causing drift in flight and requiring a crab or sideslip during approach and landing. Crosswind component is calculated from the angle between the wind direction and your track (or runway heading). The greater the angle and the stronger the wind, the larger the crosswind component. Each aircraft has a maximum demonstrated crosswind for landing, published in the AFM.
exam_tip: Crosswind = wind component blowing across your track or runway. Causes drift in flight and requires correction on approach/landing.
key_facts: Crosswind = wind component perpendicular to your track/runway
Causes drift during cruise and complicates landing
Crosswind component depends on wind angle and speed
Each aircraft has a maximum demonstrated crosswind (AFM)
Calculated using mental math, table, or navigation computer

syllabus_desc: Define true heading.
concept: True heading is the direction the aircraft's nose is pointing, measured from true north. It's different from true track because of wind  -  you need to point the nose into the wind (crab) to maintain your desired track. The angle between your heading and your track is the wind correction angle (or drift). True heading = true track ± drift. You calculate true heading during flight planning using the triangle of velocities on your navigation computer.
exam_tip: True heading = direction the nose points, measured from true north. Different from track because of wind. True heading = true track ± drift.
key_facts: True heading = direction aircraft nose points, from true north
Heading ≠ track (wind causes the difference)
True heading = true track ± wind correction angle (drift)
The crab angle compensates for crosswind
Calculated using the triangle of velocities

syllabus_desc: Define magnetic heading.
concept: Magnetic heading is the direction the aircraft's nose is pointing, measured from magnetic north. You get it by applying variation to the true heading. In NZ with ~22°E variation: magnetic heading = true heading − 22°. This is the heading you'll steer on your directional indicator (DI) or heading indicator, since these instruments are aligned to magnetic north. Your flight plan log should show both true and magnetic headings for each leg.
exam_tip: Magnetic heading = direction the nose points from magnetic north. True heading ± variation. What you steer on the DI.
key_facts: Magnetic heading = aircraft heading measured from magnetic north
Magnetic heading = true heading ± variation
What you steer on the directional indicator (DI)
NZ: magnetic heading ≈ true heading − 22°
Listed on your nav log for each leg

syllabus_desc: Define compass heading.
concept: Compass heading is the heading reading on the aircraft's magnetic compass, after accounting for both variation and deviation. It's the final step: true heading ± variation = magnetic heading ± deviation = compass heading. You use the compass heading when cross-checking or setting the DI from the magnetic compass. In practice, the DI is easier to read and doesn't suffer from the turning and acceleration errors of the magnetic compass, so you primarily fly by DI and periodically re-align it with the compass.
exam_tip: Compass heading = magnetic heading ± deviation. What the magnetic compass reads. Periodically align the DI to the compass.
key_facts: Compass heading = magnetic heading ± deviation
What the magnetic compass actually reads
Affected by aircraft deviation (from deviation card)
DI is aligned to compass heading periodically
DI precesses over time  -  check and reset every 10-15 minutes

syllabus_desc: Define drift (planned and actual).
concept: Drift is the angular difference between your heading and your track, caused by wind blowing you sideways. Planned drift (or wind correction angle) is what you calculate during flight planning  -  the amount you need to crab into the wind to maintain your desired track. Actual drift is what you observe in flight  -  the angle between where your nose is pointing and the direction you're actually tracking over the ground. If your actual drift differs from planned drift, the wind isn't what you expected, and you'll need to make an in-flight correction.
exam_tip: Drift = angle between heading and track. Planned drift = calculated WCA. Actual drift = what you observe in flight. If they differ, the wind has changed.
key_facts: Drift = angular difference between heading and track
Caused by crosswind pushing the aircraft sideways
Planned drift = wind correction angle calculated during planning
Actual drift = observed difference between heading and track in flight
Difference between planned and actual drift indicates wind change

syllabus_desc: Define track made good.
concept: Track made good (TMG) is the actual path your aircraft has flown over the ground. It may or may not be the same as your track required. If the wind is different from forecast, or your heading isn't quite right, your TMG will diverge from your planned track. You can determine your TMG by looking at where you've actually been  -  if you plotted your position fixes on the chart, the line connecting them is your TMG. The difference between TMG and track required is the track error.
exam_tip: Track made good = actual path over the ground. May differ from track required if wind was different from forecast or heading was incorrect.
key_facts: TMG = actual path flown over the ground
Determined by connecting successive position fixes
May differ from track required due to wind or heading errors
Track error = difference between TMG and track required
Monitoring TMG helps you correct navigation in flight

syllabus_desc: Define port.
concept: Port means the left side of the aircraft, looking forward. The port navigation light is red. A simple memory aid: 'port' and 'left' both have four letters. Or think of port wine, which is red  -  port light is red. When ATC says 'traffic on your port side,' they mean to your left. In NZ, circuit direction is described as left-hand or right-hand (not port/starboard), but you'll encounter port/starboard in some aviation contexts.
exam_tip: Port = left side of the aircraft. Port light is red. Memory aid: 'port' and 'left' both have four letters.
key_facts: Port = left side of the aircraft (looking forward)
Port navigation light is red
Memory aid: 'port' and 'left' both have four letters
Port wine is red  -  port light is red

syllabus_desc: Define starboard.
concept: Starboard means the right side of the aircraft, looking forward. The starboard navigation light is green. Since port is left/red, starboard is right/green  -  process of elimination. When you see another aircraft at night, if you see a red light on your right and green on your left, that aircraft is heading toward you. If you see a green light on your right, the other aircraft is moving from left to right across your path.
exam_tip: Starboard = right side of the aircraft. Starboard light is green.
key_facts: Starboard = right side of the aircraft (looking forward)
Starboard navigation light is green
Port/red on left, starboard/green on right
Navigation light positions help identify aircraft direction at night

syllabus_desc: Define wind correction angle.
concept: Wind correction angle (WCA) is the angle you need to offset your heading from your desired track to compensate for crosswind drift. If the wind is from the left, you turn your heading to the left (into the wind) by the WCA to maintain your track. The stronger the crosswind component, the larger the WCA needed. You calculate WCA using the triangle of velocities on your navigation computer. A WCA of +5° means you're crabbing 5° to the right of track; −5° means 5° to the left.
exam_tip: WCA = heading offset needed to compensate for crosswind drift. Crab into the wind. Calculated on the navigation computer.
key_facts: WCA = angle between heading and track to correct for wind
Always crab INTO the wind to maintain track
Stronger crosswind = larger WCA
Calculated using the triangle of velocities
WCA is the same as planned drift

syllabus_desc: Define deduced (dead) reckoning.
concept: Deduced reckoning (DR, commonly called 'dead reckoning') is the process of calculating your current position by starting from a known position and advancing it based on heading, speed, time, and estimated wind. It's the fundamental navigation technique  -  you work out where you should be based on where you were and how you've been flying. DR navigation accumulates errors over time (wind changes, imprecise heading), so it needs regular updates from visual fixes. Most PPL cross-country navigation combines DR with map reading (visual fixes).
exam_tip: DR = navigating by calculation from last known position + heading + speed + time + wind. Needs regular visual fixes to stay accurate.
key_facts: DR = deduced (dead) reckoning navigation
Calculate position from: known position + heading + speed + time + wind
Fundamental navigation technique
Accuracy degrades over time without visual fixes
PPL nav combines DR with map reading

syllabus_desc: Define track error (TE).
concept: Track error is the angular difference between your desired track (track required) and your actual track over the ground (track made good). If you're right of track, you have a starboard track error; left of track is a port track error. You can estimate track error visually  -  if you can see your departure point and you're off to one side of the planned track, the angle between the planned track and the line back to your departure point is the track error. The 1:60 rule helps you calculate heading corrections to get back on track.
exam_tip: Track error = angle between track required and track made good. Right of track = starboard TE. Use the 1:60 rule to correct.
key_facts: Track error = angle between planned track and actual track
Right of track = starboard track error
Left of track = port track error
Can be estimated visually or calculated
Corrected using the 1:60 rule

syllabus_desc: Define closing angle (CA).
concept: The closing angle is the additional heading change needed to not just parallel the desired track but actually converge back onto it by a specific point (usually the next waypoint or destination). If you've drifted off track, correcting by just the track error amount would only parallel the track  -  you'd fly alongside it but never get back on it. The closing angle brings you back. Total heading correction = track error + closing angle. The closing angle depends on how far along the leg you are and how far off track you are.
exam_tip: Closing angle = extra correction to converge back onto the desired track (not just parallel it). Total correction = TE + CA.
key_facts: Closing angle = heading change to converge back onto planned track
Track error correction alone only parallels the track
Total heading correction = track error + closing angle
Closing angle depends on distance remaining to the waypoint
Calculated using the 1:60 rule

syllabus_desc: Define estimated time of departure (ETD).
concept: ETD is the planned time the aircraft will depart  -  specifically, the time you expect to become airborne (wheels off the ground). It's expressed in UTC for flight plans. Your ETD drives your entire time plan: all ETAs for waypoints and destination are calculated forward from the ETD. If your actual departure is later than planned, you'll need to adjust all your ETAs accordingly. Your flight plan filed with Airways NZ will include the ETD.
exam_tip: ETD = planned time of becoming airborne. Expressed in UTC. All ETAs are calculated from the ETD.
key_facts: ETD = Estimated Time of Departure
Time the aircraft becomes airborne (not when you start taxiing)
Expressed in UTC for flight plans
All ETAs are calculated forward from the ETD
Filed with Airways NZ in the flight plan

syllabus_desc: Define actual time of departure (ATD).
concept: ATD is the actual time the aircraft becomes airborne  -  wheels off the ground. It's the real departure time as opposed to the planned ETD. If your ATD differs from your ETD, all your calculated ETAs need to be adjusted by the same amount. For example, if you planned ETD 0200 UTC but actually departed at 0210 UTC, add 10 minutes to every ETA on your nav log. You should record your ATD on your flight log and report it to ATC or in your position reports as required.
exam_tip: ATD = actual time airborne. If it differs from ETD, adjust all ETAs by the difference. Record it on your flight log.
key_facts: ATD = Actual Time of Departure (wheels off)
Record on your flight log
If ATD ≠ ETD, adjust all ETAs by the difference
Report to ATC as part of departure procedure
Expressed in UTC

syllabus_desc: Define estimated elapsed time (EET).
concept: EET is the estimated time it will take to fly a particular leg  -  from one point to another. It's calculated by dividing the leg distance by the ground speed: EET = distance ÷ GS. For example, a 60 nm leg at 120 kt ground speed gives an EET of 30 minutes. You calculate EET for each leg during flight planning and record it on your nav log. Adding the EET for each leg to your departure time gives you the ETA for that waypoint. EET is also used in your flight plan  -  total EET is the planned en-route time.
exam_tip: EET = time for a leg. Distance ÷ ground speed = EET. Used to calculate ETAs. Also used for total flight plan time.
key_facts: EET = Estimated Elapsed Time for a leg
EET = distance ÷ ground speed
Calculated for each leg during flight planning
Added to departure time to give ETA
Total EET used in the flight plan

syllabus_desc: Define estimated time of arrival (ETA).
concept: ETA is the expected time of arrival at a waypoint or destination. It's calculated by adding the EET to the ETD (or to the ETA of the previous waypoint). ETAs are expressed in UTC and recorded on your nav log. Keeping your ETAs updated in flight is a critical part of navigation  -  if you arrive at a checkpoint earlier or later than planned, recalculate the ETA for the next point. Amended ETAs should be passed to ATC when operating in controlled airspace. Your SARTIME is based on your destination ETA.
exam_tip: ETA = ETD (or previous ETA) + EET. Expressed in UTC. Update in flight if timing changes. SARTIME is based on your destination ETA.
key_facts: ETA = Estimated Time of Arrival at a waypoint/destination
ETA = ETD + EET (or previous waypoint ETA + leg EET)
Expressed in UTC on the nav log
Must be updated in flight if actual timing differs from plan
SARTIME calculated from destination ETA

syllabus_desc: Define actual time of arrival (ATA).
concept: ATA is the actual time you arrive over a waypoint or destination. You record it on your flight log as you pass each checkpoint. Comparing ATA with ETA tells you whether you're ahead of or behind schedule, and by how much  -  which helps you revise ETAs for subsequent waypoints and your destination. If your ATA at a checkpoint is 3 minutes late, your subsequent ETAs will likely be about 3 minutes late too (unless the wind changes). Record every ATA  -  it's your in-flight navigation record.
