exam_tip: ATA = actual time you arrive at a waypoint. Compare with ETA to check progress. Record on your flight log for every checkpoint.
key_facts: ATA = Actual Time of Arrival at a waypoint
Record on flight log at each checkpoint
Compare ATA with ETA to assess timing
Use the difference to revise subsequent ETAs
Expressed in UTC

syllabus_desc: Explain and apply the 1:60 rule.
concept: The 1:60 rule is one of the most useful tools in navigation: at 60 nm from a point, 1° of angle equals approximately 1 nm of displacement. So if you're 3 nm off track after flying 60 nm, your track error is about 3°. If you've only flown 30 nm and you're 3 nm off track, the track error is about 6° (because 3 nm at 30 nm = 3 × 60/30 = 6°). The formula: Track Error = (distance off track × 60) ÷ distance along track. You use this to calculate corrections in flight  -  the track error to parallel the track, plus a closing angle to regain it.
exam_tip: 1:60 rule: at 60 nm, 1° = 1 nm off track. TE = (distance off × 60) ÷ distance along track. Use for in-flight heading corrections.
key_facts: At 60 nm, 1° of angle ≈ 1 nm of displacement
Track Error (°) = (distance off track × 60) ÷ distance flown
Closing Angle (°) = (distance off track × 60) ÷ distance remaining
Total correction = TE + CA (to regain track at next waypoint)
Works for any combination of distances  -  scale with the 60 factor

syllabus_desc: Describe the six figure systems of indicating date/time groups.
concept: In aviation, time is expressed as a six-digit group: two digits for the day of the month, followed by four digits for the time in UTC. For example, 251430 means the 25th of the month at 1430 UTC (2:30 PM UTC). Sometimes you'll see the suffix 'Z' (for Zulu time, which means UTC): 251430Z. This system is used in METARs, TAFs, NOTAMs, flight plans, and all official aviation communications. In NZ, a METAR timestamped 120300Z was issued on the 12th at 0300 UTC (3:00 PM NZST or 4:00 PM NZDT).
exam_tip: Six-digit date/time: DDHHMMZ. Day + UTC time. Example: 251430Z = 25th at 1430 UTC. Used in METARs, TAFs, NOTAMs, flight plans.
key_facts: Format: DDHHMM followed by 'Z' for UTC/Zulu
DD = day of the month (01-31)
HHMM = hours and minutes in UTC
Example: 251430Z = 25th at 14:30 UTC
Used in METARs, TAFs, NOTAMs, flight plans

syllabus_desc: Define Coordinated Universal Time (UTC).
concept: UTC (Coordinated Universal Time) is the global time standard used in aviation. It's the time at the Prime Meridian (0° longitude) and doesn't change for daylight saving. All aviation communications, flight plans, METARs, TAFs, NOTAMs, and ATC operations worldwide use UTC. It's also called Zulu time (abbreviated Z). UTC ensures everyone is working from the same clock regardless of where they are. In NZ, you need to convert between local time and UTC for all aviation operations.
exam_tip: UTC = universal aviation time standard. Doesn't change for daylight saving. Also called Zulu time. Used for all aviation operations worldwide.
key_facts: UTC = Coordinated Universal Time (Zulu time)
Time at the Prime Meridian (0° longitude)
Does not change for daylight saving
Used for all aviation communications worldwide
Suffix 'Z' denotes UTC (e.g. 0300Z)

syllabus_desc: Define Standard Time (NZST).
concept: New Zealand Standard Time (NZST) is UTC plus 12 hours. So when it's 0000 UTC (midnight Zulu), it's midday (1200) in NZ. To convert from NZST to UTC: subtract 12 hours. To convert from UTC to NZST: add 12 hours. NZST applies outside the daylight saving period (first Sunday in April to last Sunday in September). For example, 0300 UTC = 1500 NZST (3:00 PM local). When filing a flight plan, remember to convert your local departure time to UTC.
exam_tip: NZST = UTC + 12 hours. To get UTC, subtract 12 from NZST. Applies outside daylight saving period.
key_facts: NZST = UTC + 12 hours
UTC = NZST − 12 hours
Applies from first Sunday in April to last Sunday in September
Example: 0300 UTC = 1500 NZST
Always convert to UTC for flight plans and aviation comms

syllabus_desc: Define Daylight Time (NZDT).
concept: New Zealand Daylight Time (NZDT) is UTC plus 13 hours. It applies during the daylight saving period  -  from the last Sunday in September to the first Sunday in April. To convert from NZDT to UTC: subtract 13 hours. So 0300 UTC during summer = 1600 NZDT (4:00 PM local). This is a common exam trap: students forget whether to use +12 or +13. Check whether daylight saving is in effect, then apply the correct offset. Getting the UTC conversion wrong could mean filing a flight plan with incorrect times.
exam_tip: NZDT = UTC + 13 hours (daylight saving time). UTC = NZDT − 13. Applies last Sunday September to first Sunday April. Don't mix up with NZST!
key_facts: NZDT = UTC + 13 hours
UTC = NZDT − 13 hours
Applies from last Sunday in September to first Sunday in April
Example: 0300 UTC = 1600 NZDT
Common exam trap: know whether to use +12 (NZST) or +13 (NZDT)

syllabus_desc: Calculate ETD and ETA in UTC given planned flight time details and reference time in NZST and/or NZDT.
concept: Here's a typical scenario: you plan to depart Hamilton at 2:30 PM local time on a summer day (NZDT) for a flight that takes 1 hour 15 minutes. Step 1: convert local time to UTC. 1430 NZDT − 13 hours = 0130 UTC. Your ETD is 0130Z. Step 2: add flight time to get ETA. 0130 + 0115 = 0245Z. If it's winter (NZST), you'd subtract 12 instead of 13. Always double-check: NZ is ahead of UTC, so UTC times are numerically smaller than local times. If your UTC time comes out larger than your local time, you've probably added instead of subtracted.
exam_tip: Convert local to UTC first (NZST −12 or NZDT −13), then add flight time. NZ is ahead of UTC, so UTC numbers are smaller.
key_facts: Step 1: Convert local departure time to UTC
NZST to UTC: subtract 12 hours
NZDT to UTC: subtract 13 hours
Step 2: Add EET to ETD(UTC) to get ETA(UTC)
NZ is ahead of UTC  -  UTC times are numerically smaller
Watch for date changes when crossing midnight UTC

syllabus_desc: Define sunrise.
concept: Sunrise is the moment when the upper edge of the sun first appears above the visible horizon. In NZ aviation, the times of sunrise are published in AIPNZ and are available through MetFlight. Sunrise marks the beginning of daylight for VFR flight purposes  -  you can fly VFR from morning civil twilight (MCT) onwards, which is before actual sunrise. Sunrise times vary with latitude, time of year, and your location within NZ  -  southern NZ gets sunrise later in winter and earlier in summer compared to northern NZ.
exam_tip: Sunrise = upper edge of sun appears above the horizon. VFR flight can commence from MCT (before sunrise). Times vary with latitude and season.
key_facts: Sunrise = upper edge of the sun appears above the horizon
Published in AIPNZ and MetFlight
VFR flight permitted from morning civil twilight (before sunrise)
Times vary with latitude, date, and location
Southern NZ has shorter winter days than northern NZ
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, AIPNZ GEN 2.7

syllabus_desc: Define sunset.
concept: Sunset is the moment when the upper edge of the sun disappears below the visible horizon. After sunset, you can continue VFR flight until evening civil twilight (ECT). Sunset times are published in AIPNZ and available from MetFlight. For PPL holders without a night rating, you must be on the ground before the end of ECT. Plan your flights to arrive with comfortable time margin before ECT  -  getting caught out after dark without a night rating is both illegal and dangerous.
exam_tip: Sunset = upper edge of sun disappears below the horizon. VFR flight can continue until ECT (after sunset). Must land before ECT without a night rating.
key_facts: Sunset = upper edge of the sun disappears below the horizon
VFR flight continues until evening civil twilight (ECT)
Published in AIPNZ and MetFlight
Must land before ECT without a night rating
Plan comfortable margins  -  don't cut it fine
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, AIPNZ GEN 2.7, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Define daylight.
concept: Daylight, in aviation terms, is the period between morning civil twilight (MCT) and evening civil twilight (ECT). This is the window during which VFR day flight is permitted. It's longer than the period between sunrise and sunset because the twilight periods at each end extend the usable daylight. The length of daylight varies significantly in NZ  -  from about 15-16 hours in midsummer to only about 9-10 hours in midwinter, and it varies between Kaitaia in the north and Invercargill in the south.
exam_tip: Daylight = MCT to ECT. Longer than sunrise-to-sunset. VFR day flights must be within this window.
key_facts: Daylight = period from MCT to ECT
Longer than sunrise-to-sunset (twilight extends both ends)
VFR day flight permitted during daylight hours only
Varies with season: ~15-16 hours summer, ~9-10 hours winter in NZ
Varies with latitude across NZ

syllabus_desc: Define twilight.
concept: Twilight is the period before sunrise and after sunset when there's still some natural illumination from the sun below the horizon. Civil twilight is the most relevant type for aviation  -  it occurs when the sun is up to 6° below the horizon. During civil twilight, there's enough light to see ground features and fly visually. There are also nautical twilight (6-12° below) and astronomical twilight (12-18° below), but for PPL purposes, civil twilight is what you need to know.
exam_tip: Twilight = period of partial illumination before sunrise/after sunset. Civil twilight (sun up to 6° below horizon) is the relevant one for VFR flight.
key_facts: Twilight = partial illumination with sun below the horizon
Civil twilight: sun 0° to 6° below horizon
Provides enough light for visual flight
Occurs before sunrise and after sunset
MCT and ECT define the start and end of VFR daylight

syllabus_desc: Define morning civil twilight (MCT).
concept: Morning civil twilight (MCT) is the time in the morning when the centre of the sun is 6° below the horizon. It's the earliest you can begin a VFR day flight. MCT occurs before sunrise  -  how long before depends on your latitude and the time of year. In NZ, MCT is typically 25-35 minutes before sunrise. MCT times are published in AIPNZ and available through MetFlight. Your latest time of departure for a VFR flight cannot be before MCT.
exam_tip: MCT = sun 6° below horizon in the morning. Earliest start for VFR day flight. Typically 25-35 minutes before sunrise in NZ. Published in AIPNZ.
key_facts: MCT = centre of sun 6° below the eastern horizon
Earliest time VFR day flight can commence
Approximately 25-35 minutes before sunrise in NZ
Published in AIPNZ and available from MetFlight
Varies with latitude and time of year
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, AIPNZ GEN 2.7

syllabus_desc: Define evening civil twilight (ECT).
concept: Evening civil twilight (ECT) is the time in the evening when the centre of the sun has dropped to 6° below the horizon. After ECT, it's officially night. For PPL holders without a night rating, you must be on the ground before ECT. ECT occurs after sunset  -  typically 25-35 minutes later in NZ. Plan your last landing with a generous buffer before ECT. Don't plan to arrive at ECT minus 5 minutes  -  things go wrong, and being caught out after dark is a serious safety issue.
exam_tip: ECT = sun 6° below horizon in the evening. Latest time for VFR day flight to end. Typically 25-35 minutes after sunset. Land well before ECT.
key_facts: ECT = centre of sun 6° below the western horizon
Latest time VFR day flight can continue
Approximately 25-35 minutes after sunset in NZ
Published in AIPNZ and available from MetFlight
Plan generous margins  -  don't cut it fine
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, AIPNZ GEN 2.7

syllabus_desc: Describe the factors that affect the times of sunrise and sunset (daylight).
concept: Several factors affect sunrise and sunset times. Latitude is the biggest  -  locations further from the equator have more extreme variation between summer and winter. In NZ, Invercargill (46°S) has much shorter winter days than Whangarei (35°S). Time of year matters too  -  the summer solstice gives the longest day, winter solstice the shortest. Longitude within the same time zone causes small differences  -  eastern locations see sunrise earlier than western ones. Elevation has a minor effect  -  higher ground sees sunrise slightly earlier and sunset slightly later. Always use the published times for your specific location.
exam_tip: Sunrise/sunset affected by: latitude (biggest factor), time of year (solstice), longitude within the time zone, and elevation. Use published times for your location.
key_facts: Latitude: further from equator = more seasonal variation
Time of year: summer solstice = longest day, winter solstice = shortest
Longitude: eastern locations see sunrise earlier within same time zone
Elevation: higher ground sees sunrise slightly earlier
NZ spans ~12° of latitude  -  significant variation north to south

syllabus_desc: Describe the factors that affect the duration of twilight.
concept: The duration of twilight depends mainly on the angle at which the sun's path crosses the horizon. Near the equator, the sun rises and sets nearly vertically, so twilight is short. At higher latitudes (like NZ), the sun's path is more oblique, so it takes longer to travel those 6° below the horizon  -  giving longer twilight. Time of year also matters  -  twilight is longest around the summer solstice in NZ. In midsummer, NZ twilight can last over 30 minutes; around the equinoxes it's shorter.
exam_tip: Twilight duration depends on latitude and time of year. Higher latitudes (like NZ) have longer twilight. Sun's oblique angle means it takes longer to reach 6° below horizon.
key_facts: Latitude: higher latitudes = longer twilight (more oblique sun path)
Time of year: twilight longest around summer solstice
Near the equator: short twilight (sun drops steeply)
NZ twilight typically 25-35 minutes
Varies between northern and southern NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the factors that affect daylight conditions.
concept: Daylight conditions (the amount of usable light for VFR flight) are affected by more than just the sun's position. Cloud cover can reduce light levels significantly  -  an overcast day in winter can be much darker than a clear day at the same sun angle. Mountain terrain creates shadows that reduce light in valleys, especially in the Southern Alps during winter when the sun is low. Precipitation (rain, snow) reduces visibility and light. Atmospheric haze and smoke also diminish light. Always consider actual conditions, not just published MCT/ECT times.
exam_tip: Daylight conditions affected by: cloud cover, terrain shadowing (especially mountain valleys), precipitation, haze, and smoke. Actual light may be less than expected.
key_facts: Cloud cover significantly reduces available light
Mountain shadows reduce light in valleys (especially winter)
Precipitation reduces visibility and light levels
Haze and smoke diminish daylight conditions
Southern Alps create significant shadow zones in winter
Actual light conditions may be worse than published MCT/ECT suggest

syllabus_desc: Derive or calculate the MCT and ECT at a given location (UTC, NZST and NZDT).
concept: MCT and ECT times are published in AIPNZ for various NZ locations, given in UTC. To use them: look up the MCT and ECT for the date and your nearest listed location. Then convert to local time by adding 12 (NZST) or 13 (NZDT) hours. For example, if MCT is published as 1815Z on a summer day, local NZDT time = 1815 + 13 = 0715 NZDT (next day). If you need times for a location between listed stations, interpolate. Always verify you're using the correct table and converting correctly  -  getting this wrong could mean flying illegally before MCT or after ECT.
exam_tip: Look up MCT/ECT from AIPNZ tables (given in UTC). Convert to local: +12 for NZST, +13 for NZDT. Interpolate between listed stations if needed.
key_facts: MCT/ECT published in AIPNZ GEN 2.7 in UTC
Convert UTC to NZST: add 12 hours
Convert UTC to NZDT: add 13 hours
Interpolate between listed stations for your location
Watch for date changes when converting
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, AIPNZ GEN 2.7

syllabus_desc: Define scale and the relationship between chart and earth distances.
concept: Scale is the ratio between a distance on the chart and the corresponding distance on the Earth's surface. If a chart has a scale of 1:500,000, it means 1 cm on the chart represents 500,000 cm (5 km) on the ground. NZ VNC charts are 1:250,000 scale, and VPC charts are 1:1,000,000. Larger scale (like 1:250,000) shows more detail but covers less area. Smaller scale (like 1:1,000,000) covers more area but with less detail. You can express scale as a ratio (1:250,000) or as a statement ('4 nm per cm').
exam_tip: Scale = ratio of chart distance to earth distance. NZ VNC = 1:250,000. VPC = 1:1,000,000. Larger scale = more detail, less area.
key_facts: Scale = chart distance ÷ earth distance
NZ VNC charts: 1:250,000 scale
NZ VPC charts: 1:1,000,000 scale
Larger scale number = less detail, more area covered
1:250,000 means 1 cm = 2.5 km on the ground
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, NZ VNC/VPC charts

syllabus_desc: Describe the appropriate use of NZ Aeronautical Charts.
concept: NZ Visual Navigation Charts (VNCs) are your primary navigation tool for cross-country VFR flying. They're 1:250,000 scale and show terrain, airspace, aerodromes, obstacles, and visual features in detail. They use a Lambert Conformal Conic projection, which means straight lines approximate great circles  -  so you can draw a straight line between two points and measure the track directly. VNC charts are updated periodically by Airways NZ/LINZ, and you must use current editions. The VPC (Visual Planning Chart) at 1:1,000,000 is used for route planning over longer distances.
exam_tip: VNC (1:250,000) for detailed VFR navigation. VPC (1:1,000,000) for route planning. VNCs use Lambert Conformal Conic projection. Always use current editions.
key_facts: VNC = Visual Navigation Chart, 1:250,000 scale
Primary chart for VFR cross-country navigation
Lambert Conformal Conic projection (straight line ≈ great circle)
Shows terrain, airspace, aerodromes, obstacles, visual features
VPC = Visual Planning Chart, 1:1,000,000, for route planning
Must use current editions  -  updated periodically
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, NZ VNC/VPC charts, AIPNZ GEN 3.2

syllabus_desc: Describe the appropriate use of Aerodrome Charts.
concept: Aerodrome charts show the layout of a specific aerodrome in detail  -  runways, taxiways, aprons, buildings, circuit directions, radio frequencies, elevation, and surrounding obstacles. In NZ, aerodrome information is published in AIPNZ Volume 4 (AD section). These charts are essential for planning arrivals and departures at unfamiliar aerodromes. They show circuit altitude, noise-sensitive areas, restricted areas, and any special procedures. Always review the aerodrome chart before flying to an aerodrome you haven't visited before.
exam_tip: Aerodrome charts (AIPNZ Vol 4) show aerodrome layout, runways, frequencies, circuit directions, obstacles, and special procedures. Review before flying to unfamiliar aerodromes.
key_facts: Shows aerodrome layout: runways, taxiways, aprons, buildings
Published in AIPNZ Volume 4 (AD section)
Includes radio frequencies, circuit altitude, circuit direction
Shows surrounding obstacles and terrain
Review before visiting unfamiliar aerodromes
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, AIPNZ Vol 4

syllabus_desc: Interpret the features and symbols of a NZ Aeronautical Chart.
concept: NZ VNC charts are packed with information. Key features include: aerodrome symbols (circles with runway alignment), airspace boundaries (blue for controlled, shown with class designation and altitudes), terrain contours and spot heights (brown), water features (blue), towns (yellow tint), roads, railways, power lines, and obstacles (with height annotations). The chart legend explains all symbols. Magnetic variation isogonals are shown as dashed magenta lines. Restricted and danger areas have specific boundary markings. Get familiar with the legend  -  it's on the chart itself and in AIPNZ.
exam_tip: Learn the VNC chart legend: aerodromes (circles), airspace (blue boundaries), terrain (brown contours), water (blue), towns (yellow), obstacles, isogonals (magenta dashed).
key_facts: Aerodrome symbols: circles with runway alignment lines
Airspace boundaries: blue lines with class and altitude labels
Terrain: brown contour lines and spot heights
Water features: blue (rivers, lakes, coastline)
Towns: yellow tint with name labels
Obstacles: marked with height AMSL and AGL
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, NZ VNC chart legend

syllabus_desc: Describe the method of indicating relief on a NZ Aeronautical Chart.
concept: Relief (terrain height) on NZ VNC charts is shown using several methods. Contour lines (brown) join points of equal elevation AMSL, with intervals typically at 500 ft or 1,000 ft. Spot heights show specific peak elevations. Layer tinting uses graduated colours  -  low elevations are green/light, higher elevations transition through brown to white for the highest mountains. The highest terrain in each grid square is shown as a spot height with an elevation box. This lets you quickly assess the highest terrain along your route for altitude planning and terrain clearance.
exam_tip: Terrain shown by: contour lines (brown), spot heights, layer tinting (green → brown → white), and maximum elevation figures. Use for altitude planning and terrain clearance.
key_facts: Contour lines (brown): join points of equal elevation AMSL
Spot heights: specific peak elevations in feet AMSL
Layer tinting: colour graduation from low (green) to high (brown/white)
Maximum elevation figures in grid squares
Used for altitude planning and terrain clearance assessment
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, NZ VNC chart legend

syllabus_desc: Interpret information from Aerodrome Charts and Operational Data pages in the AIPNZ Volume 4.
concept: AIPNZ Volume 4 contains detailed information for every NZ aerodrome. Each entry includes: aerodrome elevation, runway details (length, width, surface, strength), radio frequencies, circuit direction and altitude, lighting, fuel availability, obstacles in the vicinity, and any special procedures or restrictions. The operational data pages also show the aerodrome reference point coordinates, magnetic variation, and approach/departure procedures. For PPL planning, always check the destination and alternate aerodrome data in Vol 4  -  it tells you everything you need to know about what to expect.
exam_tip: AIPNZ Vol 4: elevation, runways, frequencies, circuit details, lighting, fuel, obstacles, special procedures. Check for every aerodrome you plan to visit.
key_facts: Aerodrome elevation (ft AMSL) and reference point coordinates
Runway details: length, width, surface type, PCN/strength
Radio frequencies: ATIS, Tower, UNICOM, AWIS
Circuit direction and altitude
Lighting, fuel availability, and hours of operation
Obstacles and special procedures/restrictions
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, AIPNZ Vol 4

syllabus_desc: Compute TAS, given CAS, pressure altitude and air temperature in degrees Celsius.
concept: To find TAS, you need three inputs: CAS (calibrated airspeed), pressure altitude, and outside air temperature (OAT). On your E6B navigation computer, set the OAT against the pressure altitude in the TAS window, then find your CAS on the inner scale and read TAS on the outer scale. As a rough guide, TAS increases about 2% per 1,000 ft above sea level over CAS. So at 6,000 ft, TAS is roughly 12% higher than CAS. You'll do this calculation for every leg during flight planning to determine your TAS for the triangle of velocities.
exam_tip: Set OAT against pressure altitude on the nav computer. Read TAS from CAS. Roughly: TAS ≈ CAS + 2% per 1,000 ft.
key_facts: Inputs: CAS, pressure altitude, OAT (°C)
Use the TAS/density altitude window on the E6B
Set OAT against pressure altitude, read TAS from CAS
Rule of thumb: TAS increases ~2% per 1,000 ft above CAS
Required for every leg during flight planning

syllabus_desc: Solve mathematical equations: multiplication (± 2%).
concept: The navigation computer's slide rule can multiply numbers quickly. Set the first number on the inner scale against the '10' index mark on the outer scale, then find the second number on the outer scale  -  the answer is on the inner scale opposite it (or vice versa depending on your computer). Accuracy should be within ±2%. This is useful for fuel calculations, distance-speed-time problems, and unit conversions during flight planning. Practise until you can use it quickly  -  the exam may not allow electronic calculators.
exam_tip: Use the slide rule scales to multiply. Set one number against the index, read the product opposite the other. Accuracy ±2%.
key_facts: Use the inner and outer scales of the navigation computer
Set first number on inner scale against index on outer scale
Read product at the second number position
Accuracy target: ±2%
Practise speed  -  exam time is limited

syllabus_desc: Solve mathematical equations: division (± 2%).
concept: For division on the navigation computer, place the dividend on the outer scale opposite the divisor on the inner scale, and read the quotient at the index. Or think of it as setting up a ratio. Division is used constantly in navigation  -  speed/distance/time calculations, fuel burn rates, conversions. Like multiplication, aim for ±2% accuracy. The key is being comfortable enough with the computer that you can set up the problem quickly and read the answer without hesitation.
exam_tip: For division: set dividend over divisor on the scales, read answer at the index. Used for speed/distance/time and fuel calculations. Accuracy ±2%.
key_facts: Set dividend on outer scale opposite divisor on inner scale
Read quotient at the index mark
Accuracy target: ±2%
Used for speed/distance/time problems
Practise until the operation is fast and natural

syllabus_desc: Solve mathematical equations: proportion (± 2%).
concept: Proportion (ratio) problems are solved by setting two known values opposite each other on the inner and outer scales, then reading the unknown value opposite the third known value. For example: if you burn 40 litres in 1 hour, how much in 2.5 hours? Set 40 against 60 (minutes), read the answer opposite 150 (minutes). This technique works for any proportional calculation  -  fuel planning, distance ratios, time estimates. All three operations (multiply, divide, proportion) use the same basic slide rule principle.
exam_tip: Set two known values opposite each other on the scales. Read the unknown opposite the third known value. Works for fuel, distance, and time ratios.
key_facts: Set known ratio on inner/outer scales
Read unknown value opposite the third known value
Accuracy target: ±2%
Used for fuel burn rates, distance ratios, time estimates
Same basic principle as multiplication and division

syllabus_desc: Derive time, speed, or distance, given two factors.
concept: The fundamental relationship: Distance = Speed × Time, or rearranged: Speed = Distance ÷ Time, and Time = Distance ÷ Speed. On the navigation computer, the speed index (usually marked with an arrow at 60) represents 1 hour. Set GS against the speed index, and you can read off distance for any time and vice versa. For example: GS = 120 kt, distance = 45 nm. Time = 45/120 × 60 = 22.5 minutes. You'll use this constantly  -  calculating leg times, estimating fuel burns, and revising ETAs in flight.
exam_tip: D = S × T. Use the speed index (60 mark) on the nav computer. Set GS at the index, read distance against time. Used for every leg calculation.
key_facts: Distance = Speed × Time
Speed = Distance ÷ Time
Time = Distance ÷ Speed
Speed index on nav computer = 60 (represents 1 hour)
Set GS at speed index, read distance/time relationships

syllabus_desc: Compute time and distance to climb/descend, given groundspeed, rate of climb/descend and height to climb/descend.
concept: To calculate climb or descent planning: first work out the time. Time to climb/descend = height change ÷ rate of climb/descend. For example, climbing 4,000 ft at 500 ft/min takes 8 minutes. Then use that time and your ground speed to find the distance covered: Distance = GS × time. At 100 kt GS, 8 minutes = 100 × 8/60 = 13.3 nm. This is important for flight planning  -  you need to know where you'll reach cruise altitude and where to begin descent, and account for the reduced ground speed during climb.
exam_tip: Time = height change ÷ rate. Distance = GS × time. Work out how far you'll travel during climb and descent for planning.
key_facts: Time to climb = height to gain ÷ rate of climb
Time to descend = height to lose ÷ rate of descent
Distance covered = ground speed × time
Account for reduced GS during climb
Used to plan top-of-climb and top-of-descent points

syllabus_desc: Compute rate of descent required to achieve a given height loss over time.
concept: If you need to descend a specific height in a specific time (or distance), you can calculate the required rate of descent. Rate of descent = height to lose ÷ time available. For example, if you need to lose 3,000 ft in 10 minutes, you need 300 ft/min descent rate. Alternatively, if you know the distance and GS: first calculate the time (distance ÷ GS), then rate = height ÷ time. This is useful when ATC requests you to be at a certain altitude by a certain point, or when planning your descent into a destination aerodrome.
exam_tip: Rate of descent = height to lose ÷ time available. Calculate time from distance and GS if needed. Plan descent so you're at the right altitude at the right point.
key_facts: Rate of descent (ft/min) = height to lose ÷ time (minutes)
Time = distance ÷ ground speed
Example: 3,000 ft in 10 min = 300 ft/min
Used for descent planning into destination
Also used when ATC assigns altitude restrictions

syllabus_desc: Compute fuel consumption, given the burn rate and time.
concept: Fuel consumed = burn rate × time. If your aircraft burns 36 litres per hour and the leg takes 45 minutes, fuel consumed = 36 × 45/60 = 27 litres. On the navigation computer, set the burn rate against the speed index (60), then read fuel consumed opposite the flight time. You'll calculate fuel for each leg during flight planning and sum them up for total trip fuel. Then add taxi fuel, reserve fuel, and any alternate fuel to get the total fuel required. Get the burn rate from your Aircraft Flight Manual.
exam_tip: Fuel = burn rate × time. Set burn rate at the 60 index on the nav computer. Sum all legs + taxi + reserve = total fuel required.
key_facts: Fuel consumed = burn rate (L/hr) × time (hours)
Use nav computer: burn rate at speed index, read fuel vs time
Get burn rate from the Aircraft Flight Manual
Calculate for each leg, then sum for trip fuel
Add taxi fuel + reserve fuel + alternate fuel = total required

syllabus_desc: Compute fuel burn rate, given the consumption and time.
concept: Burn rate = fuel consumed ÷ time. If you've used 22 litres in 40 minutes, your burn rate is 22 ÷ (40/60) = 33 litres per hour. This is useful in flight when you want to check whether your actual fuel consumption matches what you planned. Compare your actual burn rate with the AFM figure  -  if it's significantly higher than expected, you may have a problem (headwind stronger than forecast, mixture too rich, or a fuel leak). Monitoring burn rate in flight is good airmanship.
exam_tip: Burn rate = fuel consumed ÷ time. Compare actual vs planned burn rate in flight. Higher than expected may indicate a problem.
key_facts: Burn rate (L/hr) = fuel consumed ÷ time (hours)
Compare in-flight actual vs planned burn rate
Higher than expected: stronger headwind, rich mixture, or fuel issue
Lower than expected: tailwind, leaner mixture
Part of good fuel management practice

syllabus_desc: Compute fuel endurance, given the fuel quantity and burn rate.
concept: Fuel endurance = fuel remaining ÷ burn rate. If you have 100 litres on board and burn 36 litres per hour, your endurance is 100 ÷ 36 = 2 hours 47 minutes. This tells you how long you can stay airborne. Critical: this is TOTAL endurance, not usable endurance  -  you must always land with at least the legally required fuel reserves remaining. So your usable endurance = (fuel on board − required reserves) ÷ burn rate. Always know your endurance  -  it's one of the most important numbers in flight.
exam_tip: Endurance = fuel remaining ÷ burn rate. Remember to subtract reserves for usable endurance. Always know this number.
key_facts: Endurance (hours) = fuel quantity ÷ burn rate
Total endurance = all fuel on board ÷ burn rate
Usable endurance = (fuel − reserves) ÷ burn rate
Must land with at least legal minimum reserves
One of the most important numbers to know in flight

syllabus_desc: Convert between degrees Fahrenheit and Celsius (± 2%).
concept: To convert: °C = (°F − 32) × 5/9, and °F = (°C × 9/5) + 32. On the navigation computer, there's usually a conversion scale for temperature. A few key reference points: 0°C = 32°F, 15°C = 59°F (ISA sea level), 100°C = 212°F. In NZ aviation, temperature is normally given in Celsius (METARs, TAFs, OAT gauges), but some older AFMs may use Fahrenheit for performance charts. Know how to convert between the two.
exam_tip: °C = (°F − 32) × 5/9. °F = (°C × 9/5) + 32. NZ uses Celsius. Reference: 0°C = 32°F, 15°C = 59°F (ISA sea level).
key_facts: NZ uses Celsius for aviation temperature
Some older AFMs may use Fahrenheit

syllabus_desc: Convert between nautical miles, statute miles and kilometres (± 2%).
concept: The key conversions: 1 nm = 1.852 km = 1.15 statute miles. On your navigation computer, there are usually conversion scales for these. For NZ flying, the most common conversion is nm to km (for visibility comparisons) and nm to nm is just your standard distance. Mental shortcut for nm to km: double it and subtract about 15%  -  so 100 nm ≈ 185 km. For statute miles to nm: multiply by 0.87.
exam_tip: 1 nm = 1.852 km = 1.15 SM. Nav computer has conversion scales. Quick: nm × 1.85 ≈ km.
key_facts: 1 nm = 1.852 km = 1.15 statute miles
1 km = 0.54 nm = 0.621 statute miles
Navigation computer has conversion scales
Quick mental math: nm × 1.85 ≈ km
Accuracy target: ±2%

syllabus_desc: Convert between metres and feet (± 2%).
concept: 1 metre = 3.281 feet, or roughly multiply by 3.3. Going the other way: 1 foot = 0.3048 metres, or divide by 3.3. You'll need this for converting between metric and imperial heights  -  for example, terrain heights shown in metres on some references need to be converted to feet for comparison with your altitude. Quick reference: 1,000 ft ≈ 305 m, 3,000 ft ≈ 914 m, 10,000 ft ≈ 3,048 m.
exam_tip: 1 m = 3.281 ft (roughly × 3.3). 1 ft = 0.305 m (roughly ÷ 3.3). Quick: 1,000 ft ≈ 305 m.
key_facts: 1 metre = 3.281 feet
1 foot = 0.3048 metres
Quick: multiply metres by 3.3 to get feet
Reference: 1,000 ft ≈ 305 m, 3,000 ft ≈ 914 m
Accuracy target: ±2%

syllabus_desc: Convert between pounds and kilograms (± 2%).
concept: 1 kilogram = 2.205 pounds, or roughly multiply by 2.2. Going the other way: 1 pound = 0.4536 kg, or divide by 2.2. This matters for weight and balance calculations  -  some older aircraft POHs use pounds. NZ generally uses kilograms. Avgas weighs approximately 0.72 kg per litre (or about 6 lb per US gallon). Quick reference: 100 kg ≈ 220 lb, 75 kg (average person) ≈ 165 lb.
exam_tip: 1 kg = 2.205 lb (roughly × 2.2). 1 lb = 0.454 kg. NZ uses kg for W&B. Quick: 100 kg ≈ 220 lb.
key_facts: 1 kilogram = 2.205 pounds
1 pound = 0.4536 kilograms
Quick: multiply kg by 2.2 to get pounds
NZ uses kilograms for weight and balance
Avgas: approximately 0.72 kg per litre

syllabus_desc: Convert between litres, imperial and US gallons (± 2%).
concept: Three different volume measures you might encounter: 1 litre = 0.22 imperial gallons = 0.264 US gallons. Or put differently: 1 imperial gallon = 4.546 litres, 1 US gallon = 3.785 litres. Imperial gallons are larger than US gallons. NZ uses litres for fuel measurement, but some older aircraft instruments may show US gallons. Always check which gallon your fuel gauges use. The nav computer usually has a conversion scale for this.
exam_tip: NZ uses litres. 1 imperial gallon = 4.546 L. 1 US gallon = 3.785 L. Check which 'gallon' your aircraft uses!
key_facts: 1 litre = 0.22 imperial gallons = 0.264 US gallons
1 imperial gallon = 4.546 litres
1 US gallon = 3.785 litres
NZ uses litres for fuel measurement
Check which gallon type your aircraft's gauges show

syllabus_desc: Convert a volume of fuel to a mass of fuel (± 2%).
concept: Fuel volume (litres) × fuel density (kg/L) = fuel mass (kg). For Avgas 100LL, the specific gravity is approximately 0.72 kg/L (at 15°C). So 100 litres of Avgas weighs about 72 kg. This conversion matters because weight and balance uses mass (kg), but fuel gauges often show volume (litres). Remember: fuel density changes slightly with temperature  -  fuel is denser when cold and lighter when hot. For exam purposes, use 0.72 kg/L unless told otherwise. Always check the AFM for the specific gravity figure.
exam_tip: Fuel mass (kg) = volume (L) × specific gravity. Avgas ≈ 0.72 kg/L. 100 L of Avgas ≈ 72 kg. Density varies slightly with temperature.
key_facts: Fuel mass = volume × specific gravity (SG)
Avgas 100LL SG ≈ 0.72 kg/L at 15°C
100 litres of Avgas ≈ 72 kg
Fuel density decreases with increasing temperature
Check AFM for the exact SG figure to use

syllabus_desc: Calculate the headwind/tailwind component, given runway alignment and wind velocity.
concept: The headwind or tailwind component is the portion of the wind acting along the runway direction. Headwind component = wind speed × cos(angle between wind and runway). For a direct headwind (wind straight down the runway), the HW component equals the full wind speed. At 30° off, it's about 87% of the wind. At 60° off, it's 50%. At 90° (crosswind only), the HW component is zero. You can calculate this with the nav computer, a crosswind component chart, or the simple rule: 30° off = full wind × cos30° ≈ 0.87.
exam_tip: HW/TW component = wind speed × cos(angle off runway). Direct: 100%. 30° off: 87%. 60° off: 50%. 90° off: 0%.
key_facts: HW component = wind speed × cos(angle between wind and runway)
Direct headwind (0° off): 100% of wind speed
30° off: ~87% of wind speed
60° off: ~50% of wind speed
90° crosswind: 0% headwind component
Wind from behind the runway = tailwind

syllabus_desc: Calculate the crosswind component, given runway alignment and wind velocity.
concept: The crosswind component is the portion of the wind acting across the runway. Crosswind component = wind speed × sin(angle between wind and runway). For a direct crosswind (90° off the runway), the XW component equals the full wind speed. At 60° off, it's about 87%. At 30° off, it's 50%. At 0° (straight headwind), there's no crosswind. Compare the crosswind component to your aircraft's maximum demonstrated crosswind in the AFM. If it exceeds the limit, choose a different runway or wait for conditions to change.
exam_tip: XW component = wind speed × sin(angle off runway). 30° off: 50%. 60° off: 87%. 90° off: 100%. Compare against aircraft XW limit.
key_facts: XW component = wind speed × sin(angle between wind and runway)
Direct crosswind (90° off): 100% of wind speed
60° off: ~87% of wind speed
30° off: ~50% of wind speed
Direct headwind (0° off): 0% crosswind
Compare with aircraft max demonstrated crosswind (AFM)

syllabus_desc: Identify and label the three vectors of the triangle of velocities.
concept: The triangle of velocities is the foundation of navigation calculations. It has three vectors: the air vector (heading and TAS  -  where the aircraft's nose points and how fast it moves through the air), the wind vector (wind direction and speed  -  where the wind is coming from and how fast), and the ground vector (track and ground speed  -  the actual path over the ground and the speed along it). When you combine the air vector and the wind vector, you get the ground vector. The navigation computer solves this triangle for you.
exam_tip: Three vectors: Air (heading + TAS), Wind (direction + speed), Ground (track + GS). Air + Wind = Ground. The nav computer solves this.
key_facts: Air vector = heading + TAS (aircraft through the air)
Wind vector = wind direction and speed
Ground vector = track + ground speed (actual path over ground)
Air vector + Wind vector = Ground vector
Navigation computer solves the triangle of velocities

syllabus_desc: Solve triangle of velocity problems: heading and track (± 2°).
concept: Given TAS, wind velocity, and either the desired track or heading, you can solve for the other using the triangle of velocities on your navigation computer. Set the wind on the wind side of the computer (wind direction under true index, mark wind speed), then set TAS and read off the heading to maintain the desired track, or read the track that results from a given heading. The drift angle (difference between heading and track) tells you how much the wind is pushing you off course. Accuracy target: ±2°.
exam_tip: Use the wind side of the nav computer. Set wind, then TAS. Read heading from track (or track from heading). Drift = heading − track. Accuracy ±2°.
key_facts: Set wind direction under true index on nav computer
Mark wind speed above the centre dot
Set TAS, read heading for desired track
Drift angle = difference between heading and track
Accuracy target: ±2°

syllabus_desc: Solve triangle of velocity problems: TAS and GS (± 2 kts).
concept: The triangle of velocities also gives you ground speed. Once you've set up the wind and heading/track on the nav computer, read GS from the centre line of the ground speed grid. GS = TAS adjusted for the wind component along your track. A headwind reduces GS below TAS; a tailwind increases it. You need accurate GS for calculating leg times and ETAs. If GS differs from what you planned, your ETAs will be wrong and need revising.
exam_tip: Read GS from the nav computer after setting wind, TAS, and track. GS = TAS ± along-track wind component. Accuracy ±2 kt.
key_facts: Ground speed read from the centre of the wind grid
GS = TAS adjusted for along-track wind component
Headwind: GS < TAS; Tailwind: GS > TAS
Accurate GS essential for leg time and ETA calculations
Accuracy target: ±2 kt

syllabus_desc: Solve triangle of velocity problems: wind velocity (± 3°/± 3 kts).
concept: If you know your heading, TAS, track (from map reading), and ground speed (from timing between fixes), you can work backwards to find the actual wind. On the nav computer: set track and GS, then mark the point. Set heading and TAS  -  the displacement between the mark and the centre dot gives you the wind speed and direction. This is how you determine actual wind in flight  -  extremely useful for revising your navigation if the wind isn't as forecast.
exam_tip: Work backwards from known heading, TAS, track, and GS to find actual wind. Useful for in-flight wind checks. Accuracy ±3°/±3 kt.
key_facts: Given heading, TAS, track, and GS → solve for wind
Set track/GS on nav computer, mark position
Set heading/TAS  -  displacement = wind vector
Used to check actual wind vs forecast in flight
Accuracy target: ±3° direction, ±3 kt speed

syllabus_desc: Solve triangle of velocity problems: drift (± 1°).
concept: Drift is the angle between heading and track  -  the sideways displacement caused by crosswind. On the nav computer, drift is read directly from the wind side once you've set up the problem. Positive drift (starboard) means the wind is pushing you to the right of your heading; negative (port) means to the left. Your wind correction angle equals the drift but in the opposite direction  -  you crab into the wind. Planned drift should be compared with actual drift in flight to confirm the wind is as expected.
exam_tip: Drift = angle between heading and track. Read from the nav computer. Crab into the wind by the drift angle. Accuracy ±1°.
key_facts: Drift = angle between heading and track
Caused by crosswind component
Starboard drift = wind pushing right
Port drift = wind pushing left
WCA equals drift but in opposite direction (crab into wind)
Accuracy target: ±1°

syllabus_desc: 1:60 rule computations.
concept: The 1:60 rule lets you estimate track error and corrections mentally in flight. The principle: at 60 nm, 1° of angle produces approximately 1 nm of offset. The formulas: Track Error (°) = (nm off track × 60) ÷ nm flown. Closing Angle (°) = (nm off track × 60) ÷ nm remaining. Total heading change to regain track at the next waypoint = TE + CA. Example: after 40 nm, you're 2 nm left of track with 20 nm to go. TE = (2 × 60)/40 = 3°. CA = (2 × 60)/20 = 6°. Total correction = 9° right. Practise these calculations until they're second nature.
exam_tip: TE = (off track × 60) ÷ distance flown. CA = (off track × 60) ÷ distance remaining. Total correction = TE + CA. Practise until automatic.
key_facts: 1° at 60 nm ≈ 1 nm of offset
TE (°) = (nm off track × 60) ÷ nm flown
CA (°) = (nm off track × 60) ÷ nm remaining to waypoint
Total heading correction = TE + CA
Apply correction in the direction back toward the desired track

syllabus_desc: Estimate a heading change, using the 1:60 rule (± 2°).
concept: When you discover you're off track during flight, use the 1:60 rule to calculate the heading change needed to regain your track by the next waypoint. Estimate how far off track you are (by comparing ground features with your planned track on the chart), then apply: TE = (off track × 60) / distance flown, CA = (off track × 60) / distance remaining. The total heading change = TE + CA, applied toward the desired track. If you're right of track, turn left; if left of track, turn right.
exam_tip: Calculate TE and CA using 1:60. Total change = TE + CA. Right of track → turn left. Left of track → turn right. Accuracy ±2°.
key_facts: Estimate distance off track from chart/ground reference
TE (°) = (off track × 60) ÷ distance flown
CA (°) = (off track × 60) ÷ distance remaining
Total heading change = TE + CA toward desired track
Right of track: correct left. Left of track: correct right

syllabus_desc: Estimate a heading change, using drift lines (± 2°).
concept: Drift lines are pre-drawn on your chart during planning  -  typically at 5° and 10° either side of your planned track. If you arrive at a checkpoint and find you're on the 5° left drift line, you know your actual drift is 5° more than planned from the left. Double the track error gives you the correction to parallel the track; add a closing angle to regain it. Some pilots prefer this visual method over mental arithmetic  -  it's quick to see which drift line you're near and apply the correction immediately.
exam_tip: Pre-draw 5° and 10° drift lines on your chart. At a checkpoint, see which line you're near. Double the TE to regain track. Visual and fast.
key_facts: Draw 5° and 10° drift lines either side of planned track
At checkpoints, identify which drift line you're nearest
Drift line position tells you the track error directly
Double the TE to correct back to track at the next waypoint
Quick visual method  -  no mental arithmetic needed

syllabus_desc: Estimate a heading to make good a reciprocal track.
concept: If you need to return along your track (reciprocal), you can't simply turn 180° and expect to retrace your path  -  the wind that pushed you one way will now push you the other. For the reciprocal track heading: take your current heading, add or subtract 180°, then double the drift and apply it on the opposite side. If you had 5° starboard drift outbound, apply 10° port correction for the reciprocal. This works because the crosswind effect reverses when you turn around, so you need to compensate in the other direction.
exam_tip: Reciprocal heading ≠ heading ± 180°. You need to double the drift and apply it on the opposite side to account for reversed wind effect.
key_facts: Reciprocal heading = current heading ± 180° ± double the drift
Drift reverses direction when you turn around
Outbound starboard drift → inbound port drift (and vice versa)
Double the drift to convert from outbound to inbound WCA
Quick method: reciprocal of heading ± 2× drift on opposite side

syllabus_desc: Estimate a ground speed change.
concept: If you arrive at a checkpoint earlier or later than planned, the wind component along your track is different from forecast. You can estimate your actual ground speed by timing between two known features and dividing the distance by the time. Once you have the actual GS, use it to recalculate ETAs for subsequent waypoints. A simple check: if you planned 20 minutes for a leg and it took 24 minutes, your actual GS is 20/24 of your planned GS. Revise all subsequent leg times accordingly.
exam_tip: Time between known features to find actual GS. If arrival is early, GS is higher than planned (stronger tailwind or weaker headwind). Revise subsequent ETAs.
key_facts: Time between two known points to measure actual GS
Actual GS = distance between points ÷ time taken
Early arrival: GS higher than planned
Late arrival: GS lower than planned
Revise ETAs for all subsequent waypoints

syllabus_desc: Estimate an ETA change, using proportional method.
concept: The proportional method is the quickest way to revise your ETA in flight. If you arrive at a checkpoint X minutes early or late, your ETA at the destination will change in proportion to the remaining distance. For example, if you're 4 minutes late at the halfway point, you'll be approximately 4 minutes late at the destination too. If you're 4 minutes late at the one-third point, you'll be approximately 8 minutes late at the destination (4 × 2). The formula: ETA revision = (time error × remaining distance) ÷ distance flown.
exam_tip: Proportional method: ETA revision = (time error × distance remaining) ÷ distance flown. Quick for in-flight ETA updates.
key_facts: ETA revision = (time error × distance remaining) ÷ distance flown
Late at halfway = same amount late at destination
Late at one-third = twice as late at destination
Quick mental method for in-flight ETA revisions
Apply revision to all subsequent waypoint ETAs

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of variations in heading speed and altitude.
concept: Changes in heading (direction) and airspeed affect your navigation. If you're forced to alter heading to avoid weather or terrain, your track changes and you'll need to replan to get back on course. If you fly faster or slower than planned (different power setting, icing, etc.), your ground speed changes and ETAs shift. Altitude changes matter too  -  TAS increases with altitude (approximately 2% per 1,000 ft), so climbing changes your GS and time calculations. If you divert to a lower altitude to stay below cloud, your TAS drops and legs take longer.
exam_tip: Heading changes alter your track  -  replan to regain course. Speed/altitude changes affect TAS and therefore GS and ETAs. Lower altitude = lower TAS = longer legs.
key_facts: Heading changes affect track  -  may need to replan route
Speed changes alter GS and therefore ETAs
TAS increases ~2% per 1,000 ft  -  altitude matters
Lower altitude = lower TAS = longer leg times = more fuel
Recalculate ETAs after any significant change

syllabus_desc: Describe the limitations affecting navigation in conditions of reduced visibility.
concept: Reduced visibility makes visual navigation much harder. You can't see features as far ahead, recognition of checkpoints is delayed, and you have less time to identify ground features before they pass beneath you. In NZ, weather closing in around the ranges (Kaimai, Tararua, Southern Alps) can reduce visibility rapidly. Low cloud forces you lower, which reduces your visual range further and increases terrain risk. If visibility drops below VFR minima (5 km for controlled airspace, 5 km or clear of cloud in Class G below 3,000 ft AGL), you must not continue VFR. Plan escape routes and know your 'turn back' point.
exam_tip: Reduced visibility limits feature recognition, delays checkpoint identification, and forces lower altitudes. Know VFR minima, plan escape routes, and don't press on into deteriorating conditions.
key_facts: Reduced visibility limits distance at which features can be identified
Delays checkpoint recognition  -  less time to plan corrections
Low cloud forces lower altitudes  -  less visual range, more terrain risk
NZ mountain areas (Kaimai, Tararua, Alps) can close in rapidly
VFR minima: 5 km visibility in most situations
Plan escape routes and know your 'turn back' point
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, CAR Part 91.301

syllabus_desc: List the factors to be considered when selecting a VFR cross-country navigation route.
concept: Route selection involves balancing many factors: terrain (avoid high terrain where possible, especially in marginal weather), weather (current and forecast along route), airspace (know what controlled and restricted areas you'll cross), distance (shorter isn't always better if terrain is worse), availability of forced landing areas (especially over water or bush), visual features for navigation (rivers, roads, coastlines make better navigation references than featureless bush), aerodrome alternates along the route, and NOTAMs for anything unusual. In NZ, think about the mountain ranges  -  many routes go around rather than over the ranges.
exam_tip: Consider: terrain, weather, airspace, distance, forced landing areas, visual features for nav, alternate aerodromes, and NOTAMs. In NZ, plan around mountain ranges.
key_facts: Terrain: avoid high terrain in marginal weather
Weather: current and forecast conditions along route
Airspace: controlled and restricted areas
Forced landing areas: avoid extended overwater or bush segments
Visual features: roads, rivers, coastlines aid navigation
Alternate aerodromes available along route
NOTAMs for route and destination
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: List the factors to be considered when selecting altitudes at which to fly in the cruise.
concept: Cruise altitude selection involves: terrain clearance (minimum safe altitude for the route), semi-circular rule (VFR cruising levels based on magnetic track), wind (higher altitude may mean stronger tailwind or lighter headwind  -  check the winds aloft forecast), cloud base (must remain VMC  -  below cloud in clear air), turbulence (thermal turbulence is worse at lower levels on hot days; mountain wave may be a factor near the Alps), airspace restrictions (stay below controlled airspace ceilings), aircraft performance (service ceiling, oxygen requirements above 10,000 ft), and passenger comfort.
exam_tip: Consider: terrain clearance, VFR cruising levels (semi-circular rule), wind aloft, cloud base, turbulence, airspace, aircraft performance, and oxygen requirements.
key_facts: Terrain clearance: minimum safe altitude for the route
VFR cruising levels based on magnetic track direction
Wind: check winds aloft forecast for optimal altitude
Cloud base: must maintain VMC
Turbulence: thermal, mechanical, or mountain wave
Airspace: stay below controlled airspace if not cleared
Oxygen required above 10,000 ft for prolonged flight
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, CAR Part 91.311, AIPNZ ENR 1.7

syllabus_desc: List the factors to be considered when selecting alternate routes and destination alternates.
concept: You should always have a Plan B. Alternate routes should avoid the worst terrain (go around the ranges rather than over), provide better weather options, and have suitable aerodromes along the way. Destination alternates should be: within fuel range (including reserves), likely to have better weather than the primary destination, have adequate facilities (runway length, fuel, if needed), and be reasonably close. In NZ, weather can close in fast  -  having a nominated alternate aerodrome with known good weather is good airmanship. CAR Part 91 may require an alternate for certain operations.
exam_tip: Always have a Plan B. Alternates should be within fuel range, likely to have better weather, with adequate facilities. NZ weather changes fast.
key_facts: Alternate routes: avoid worst terrain, better weather options
Destination alternate: within fuel range including reserves
Alternate should likely have better weather than primary destination
Adequate facilities: runway length, fuel availability
NZ weather can close in rapidly  -  always have an escape plan
Check NOTAMs for alternates too
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Mark the departure aerodrome, turning points, and destination aerodrome on a map.
concept: Start your chart preparation by clearly marking the departure aerodrome, all turning points, and the destination with distinct symbols  -  circles or triangles work well. Label each point with its name. This gives you the skeleton of your route at a glance. For NZ cross-country flights, also mark any significant aerodromes along the route that could serve as alternates. Make your marks clear but not so large that they obscure chart detail you'll need for map reading.
exam_tip: Mark departure, turning points, and destination clearly on the chart. Label each. Also mark potential alternate aerodromes along the route.
key_facts: Mark departure aerodrome with a clear symbol
Mark each turning point and label with the name
Mark destination aerodrome
Mark alternate aerodromes along the route
Keep marks clear but don't obscure chart detail

syllabus_desc: Mark tracks on a map.
concept: Draw a straight line between each successive pair of points (departure → first turning point → second turning point → destination). This is your planned track. Use a fine pen or pencil so you don't obscure chart features beneath the line. Measure and annotate each track with the true track direction and the leg distance. Some pilots use different coloured pens for the track line and annotations. The track line is your reference for in-flight navigation  -  you'll compare your actual position against this line throughout the flight.
exam_tip: Draw straight lines between successive waypoints. Annotate each leg with true track and distance. Use fine lines to avoid obscuring chart features.
key_facts: Draw straight track lines between consecutive waypoints
Use a fine pen/pencil  -  don't obscure chart features
Annotate each leg with true track (°T) and distance (nm)
Track line is your in-flight navigation reference
Consider using coloured pens for clarity

syllabus_desc: Mark heading change markings, either 1:60 or drift lines.
concept: Along each leg, mark heading change references to help with in-flight corrections. The two main methods are: drift lines (draw lines at 5° and 10° either side of your planned track from each waypoint  -  if you find yourself on a drift line, you know exactly how far off track you are), or 1:60 distance markers (mark distances at regular intervals like 10 nm so you can quickly calculate track error using the 1:60 rule). Many pilots prefer drift lines because they're instantly visual  -  no mental arithmetic needed at the checkpoint.
exam_tip: Draw drift lines (5° and 10° either side of track) or distance markers along each leg. Drift lines give instant visual track error assessment.
key_facts: Draw 5° and 10° drift lines from departure/turning points
Drift lines give instant visual assessment of track error
Alternatively, mark distance intervals (e.g. every 10 nm) along track
Distance marks allow 1:60 calculations at any point
Many pilots prefer drift lines for speed and simplicity

syllabus_desc: Mark ETA amendment markings.
concept: Mark time checkpoints along each leg  -  typically at 10-minute or 10-nm intervals. These let you quickly assess your ground speed and revise ETAs in flight. Some pilots mark '¼-way,' 'halfway,' and '¾-way' points along longer legs. When you pass a time checkpoint, compare your actual time with the planned time  -  any difference tells you your GS is different from planned, and you can use the proportional method to revise your destination ETA. These markings turn your chart into an in-flight management tool, not just a navigation reference.
exam_tip: Mark time/distance checkpoints along each leg (e.g. 10-min intervals or ¼/½/¾ points). Compare actual vs planned times to revise ETAs.
key_facts: Mark regular intervals along each leg (10 min or 10 nm)
Or mark ¼-way, halfway, and ¾-way points
Compare actual passing time vs planned time at each mark
Use differences to revise ETAs (proportional method)
Turns your chart into an in-flight management tool

syllabus_desc: Fold a map in a manner appropriate for a VFR cross-country flight.
concept: Fold your chart so that your entire route is visible without unfolding in the cockpit. For a VNC chart, this usually means folding it into a strip that shows the track from departure to destination, with enough margin on either side to see features for map reading. The fold should be small enough to manage on your kneepad or in the confined cockpit space, but large enough that you don't lose situational awareness of terrain and features beside your track. Don't fold through important information like aerodromes or airspace boundaries if you can avoid it.
exam_tip: Fold the chart into a manageable strip showing your entire route with margins for map reading. Must be usable on a kneepad in a confined cockpit.
key_facts: Fold chart to show entire route without unfolding
Strip fold along the track direction works best
Include margins either side for map reading
Small enough for kneepad use in the cockpit
Avoid folding through critical information

syllabus_desc: Complete a navigation log: TAS.
concept: For each leg, calculate your TAS from the planned CAS, cruise altitude (pressure altitude), and expected temperature at that altitude. Use your navigation computer: set OAT against pressure altitude in the TAS window, then read TAS from CAS on the scales. The AFM gives you the expected CAS for your planned power setting. Remember TAS increases about 2% per 1,000 ft, so at 6,500 ft your TAS will be roughly 13% higher than your CAS. Record the TAS for each leg on your nav log.
exam_tip: Calculate TAS for each leg using CAS + pressure altitude + OAT on the navigation computer. Record on your nav log.
key_facts: TAS calculated from CAS, pressure altitude, and OAT
Use the TAS window on the E6B navigation computer
CAS from AFM for planned power setting
TAS increases ~2% per 1,000 ft above sea level
Record TAS on the nav log for each leg

syllabus_desc: Complete a navigation log: tracks.
concept: Measure the true track for each leg directly from the chart using a protractor aligned with the nearest meridian at the mid-point of the leg. Record the true track on your nav log, then apply magnetic variation to get the magnetic track. In NZ, subtract the easterly variation (typically 20-23°E). The magnetic track is needed for selecting the correct VFR cruising level (semi-circular rule) and for the triangle of velocities calculation.
exam_tip: Measure true track from the chart at the mid-meridian. Apply variation to get magnetic track. Magnetic track determines your cruising level.
key_facts: Measure true track using protractor at mid-meridian of leg
Record true track on nav log
Apply variation: magnetic track = true track − variation (E)
Magnetic track determines VFR cruising level
NZ variation approximately 20-23°E

syllabus_desc: Complete a navigation log: estimated wind velocities.
concept: Get the forecast wind at your planned cruise altitude from the area forecast or winds aloft data available through MetFlight or the NZ Met Service. Wind is given as direction/speed (e.g. 270/20  -  from the west at 20 knots). Remember: winds aloft forecasts give the direction in degrees TRUE (not magnetic like surface winds). Record the W/V for each leg on your nav log. If your route crosses different wind zones, use the appropriate wind for each leg. Winds aloft are forecast for specific altitudes  -  interpolate if your cruise level is between forecast levels.
exam_tip: Get winds aloft from MetFlight or area forecast. Winds aloft are in degrees TRUE. Record W/V for each leg. Interpolate between altitude levels if needed.
key_facts: Obtain winds aloft forecast from MetFlight / NZ Met Service
Winds aloft direction is in degrees TRUE
Format: direction/speed (e.g. 270/20 = from west at 20 kt)
Record W/V for each leg on the nav log
Interpolate between forecast altitude levels if needed
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, MetFlight, NZ Met Service

syllabus_desc: Complete a navigation log: headings.
concept: Calculate the heading for each leg using the triangle of velocities on your navigation computer. Enter the wind velocity, TAS, and desired track  -  the computer gives you the heading (true) and the drift angle. Record the true heading, then apply variation to get magnetic heading, and deviation (from the aircraft's deviation card) to get compass heading. The magnetic heading is what you'll steer on the DI. Record all three (true, magnetic, compass) on your nav log.
exam_tip: Solve the triangle of velocities for each leg to get heading. True heading ± variation = magnetic heading (for the DI) ± deviation = compass heading.
key_facts: Use triangle of velocities: wind + TAS + track → heading
Record true heading on the nav log
Apply variation for magnetic heading (steer on DI)
Apply deviation for compass heading
Heading differs from track by the drift angle (WCA)

syllabus_desc: Complete a navigation log: groundspeeds.
concept: Ground speed for each leg comes directly from the triangle of velocities solution  -  the same calculation that gives you heading also gives you GS. Record it on your nav log. GS is critical because it determines your leg times and therefore your ETAs and fuel calculations. If the wind is significantly different from forecast (you'll know when you get in-flight timing data), you'll need to revise your GS and recalculate ETAs and fuel requirements.
exam_tip: GS comes from the triangle of velocities. Record it  -  it determines leg times, ETAs, and fuel calculations.
key_facts: GS obtained from triangle of velocities calculation
Read from the centre line on the nav computer wind side
Determines leg times (EET) and ETAs
Affects fuel planning calculations
Revise in flight if actual GS differs from planned

syllabus_desc: Complete a navigation log: distances.
concept: Measure the distance for each leg from the chart using the latitude scale (1 minute of latitude = 1 nm). Use dividers for accuracy. Record the leg distance on your nav log. Also calculate cumulative distances from departure  -  this helps with in-flight position awareness. For legs with a climb or descent segment, you might note the distance for the climb/descent portion separately (calculated from time and GS during climb/descent) to get more accurate fuel calculations.
exam_tip: Measure each leg distance from the chart using the latitude scale. Record on nav log. Also note cumulative distances from departure.
key_facts: Measure leg distance using chart latitude scale
1 minute of latitude = 1 nm
Use dividers for accuracy
Record leg distance and cumulative distance on nav log
Note climb/descent distances separately if significant

syllabus_desc: Complete a navigation log: EET.
concept: Calculate the Estimated Elapsed Time for each leg: EET = distance ÷ ground speed. Use the navigation computer or mental math. Record EET on the nav log in minutes. For example, a 75 nm leg at 120 kt GS gives EET = 75/120 × 60 = 37.5 minutes. Round sensibly  -  to the nearest minute for practical use. Sum all leg EETs to get the total en route time for your flight plan. EET is also used with the burn rate to calculate leg fuel consumption.
exam_tip: EET = distance ÷ GS. Calculate for each leg in minutes. Sum for total en route time. Also used for fuel calculations.
key_facts: EET = distance ÷ ground speed (result in hours, convert to minutes)
Record on nav log for each leg
Sum all EETs for total en route time
Used to calculate ETAs for each waypoint
Also used to calculate fuel burn for each leg

syllabus_desc: Complete a navigation log: ETA.
concept: Calculate the ETA for each waypoint by adding the leg EET to the previous waypoint's ETA (or the ETD for the first leg). ETAs are expressed in UTC. For example: ETD 0200Z, first leg EET 25 minutes → ETA first waypoint 0225Z, second leg EET 32 minutes → ETA second waypoint 0257Z, and so on. Record each ETA on your nav log. In flight, compare your ATA at each checkpoint with the ETA to assess whether you're on schedule, ahead, or behind.
exam_tip: ETA = previous ETA + leg EET. Express in UTC. Record on nav log. Compare with ATA in flight to check timing.
key_facts: ETA = previous waypoint ETA + leg EET
First ETA = ETD + first leg EET
Expressed in UTC (Zulu time)
Record on nav log for every waypoint
Compare ATA vs ETA in flight to assess progress

syllabus_desc: Complete a navigation log: SARTIME.
concept: SARTIME (Search and Rescue Time) is the time after which, if you haven't arrived or cancelled your flight plan, search and rescue will be activated. In NZ, SARTIME = destination ETA + 30 minutes (or another time you nominate, but no more than 24 hours after ETD). You must either arrive and close your flight plan, or extend your SARTIME before it expires. If you don't, SAR operations begin  -  which wastes resources and costs money. Record SARTIME on your nav log. It's one of the last things you calculate, based on your destination ETA.
exam_tip: SARTIME = destination ETA + 30 minutes (default). Must close flight plan or extend before SARTIME. Failure to do so activates SAR.
key_facts: SARTIME = destination ETA + 30 minutes (default in NZ)
Can nominate a different SARTIME (max 24 hours after ETD)
Must close flight plan or extend SARTIME before it expires
Failure to close activates Search and Rescue operations
Record SARTIME on your nav log
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, CAR Part 91.311, AIPNZ ENR 1.10

syllabus_desc: Calculate the latest time of departure for a given VFR cross-country flight or a given leg.
concept: To calculate the latest time of departure, work backwards from the constraint: Latest ETD = ECT (evening civil twilight) at the destination minus total flight time minus any buffer. For example, if ECT at your destination is 0630Z, total flight time is 2 hours 15 minutes, and you want a 15-minute buffer: latest ETD = 0630 − 0215 − 0015 = 0400Z. Convert to local time for planning. Always consider that conditions might deteriorate, requiring a diversion  -  build in margins. Some aerodromes have operating hours too (check AIPNZ Vol 4).
exam_tip: Latest ETD = ECT at destination − total flight time − safety buffer. Convert to local time. Factor in aerodrome operating hours and weather margins.
key_facts: Latest ETD = ECT at destination − total flight time − buffer
ECT from AIPNZ tables for destination location
Include taxi, climb, cruise, descent, and approach time
Build in safety margins for weather or diversions
Check aerodrome operating hours in AIPNZ Vol 4
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, AIPNZ GEN 2.7, AIPNZ Vol 4

syllabus_desc: Derive, from an Aircraft Flight Manual, the fuel consumption rate for a given leg.
concept: The Aircraft Flight Manual (AFM) or Pilot Operating Handbook (POH) contains fuel consumption data  -  typically as litres per hour (L/h) for different power settings and altitudes. For cruise planning, look up the fuel consumption at your planned power setting and altitude. Higher altitude and lean mixture give better fuel economy. The AFM might present this as tables or graphs. Use the figure that matches your planned conditions. If between listed values, interpolate. This fuel consumption rate drives all your fuel calculations.
exam_tip: Look up fuel burn rate in the AFM/POH for your planned power setting and altitude. Higher altitude + lean mixture = better economy. Interpolate between listed values.
key_facts: AFM/POH provides fuel consumption rates (L/h)
Look up rate for planned power setting and altitude
Higher altitude and lean mixture reduce fuel burn
Data presented as tables or graphs
Interpolate between listed values if needed

syllabus_desc: Calculate the expected fuel burn on a given leg.
concept: Leg fuel = burn rate × leg time. Use the EET calculated from your nav log and the burn rate from the AFM. For example: burn rate 36 L/h, leg time 25 minutes → fuel = 36 × 25/60 = 15 litres. Calculate this for every leg including climb (which has a higher burn rate  -  use the AFM climb fuel flow figure). Sum all leg fuels for total trip fuel. On the navigation computer, set burn rate at the speed index (60), then read fuel consumed opposite the leg time.
exam_tip: Leg fuel = burn rate × leg time. Use AFM climb fuel rate for climb segments. Sum all legs for trip fuel.
key_facts: Leg fuel = burn rate (L/h) × leg time (hours or minutes)
Use higher burn rate for climb segments (from AFM)
Calculate for every leg including climb and descent
Sum all legs for total trip fuel
Nav computer: burn rate at 60 index, read fuel vs time

syllabus_desc: Calculate the minimum fuel required on a given VFR cross-country flight.
concept: Minimum fuel required = taxi fuel + trip fuel + reserve fuel + alternate fuel (if applicable). Taxi fuel is typically 5-10 litres depending on the aircraft and taxi distance. Trip fuel is the sum of all leg fuels. Reserve fuel is the legal minimum (see 6.46.8). Alternate fuel is the fuel to fly from your destination to your alternate aerodrome if you nominate one. Always calculate minimum fuel required and compare with your available fuel. If available fuel is less than minimum required, you cannot go  -  either reduce the trip or add a fuel stop.
exam_tip: Minimum fuel = taxi + trip + reserve + alternate. If available fuel < minimum required, the trip can't proceed as planned.
key_facts: Minimum fuel = taxi fuel + trip fuel + reserve fuel + alternate fuel
Taxi fuel: typically 5-10 litres (depends on aircraft/distance)
Trip fuel: sum of all leg fuel burns
Reserve fuel: legal minimum per CAR Part 91
Alternate fuel: if alternate aerodrome nominated
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: State the legal minimum fuel reserves required on a VFR cross-country flight.
concept: Under NZ CAR Part 91, the minimum fuel reserve for VFR flight is: fuel to fly for 30 minutes at normal cruise consumption for day VFR, and 45 minutes for night VFR. This reserve must remain in the tanks when you land at your destination (or alternate). It's a legal requirement, not a suggestion. Many experienced pilots carry more than the minimum  -  weather delays, diversions, holding, and unexpected headwinds can all eat into your fuel. The old saying 'the only time you have too much fuel is when you're on fire' has some truth to it.
exam_tip: NZ legal minimum reserves: 30 minutes fuel for VFR day, 45 minutes for VFR night, at normal cruise consumption. This is a MINIMUM  -  carry more.
key_facts: VFR day: 30 minutes reserve at normal cruise consumption
VFR night: 45 minutes reserve at normal cruise consumption
Reserve fuel must remain when landing at destination
This is a legal minimum under CAR Part 91
Good practice: carry more than the minimum
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, CAR Part 91.521

syllabus_desc: Calculate the maximum fuel endurance.
concept: Maximum endurance = total usable fuel ÷ fuel consumption rate at the most economical cruise setting. Usable fuel is the fuel that can actually be fed to the engine  -  not the same as total tank capacity (there's always some unusable fuel in the bottom of the tanks). The AFM tells you both total capacity and usable capacity. For maximum endurance, use the lowest fuel consumption power setting from the AFM. This gives you the theoretical maximum time you can stay airborne. Subtract reserves for practical usable endurance.
exam_tip: Max endurance = usable fuel ÷ minimum burn rate (from AFM). Remember usable ≠ total capacity. Subtract reserves for practical endurance.
key_facts: Max endurance = usable fuel ÷ minimum burn rate
Usable fuel ≠ total tank capacity (some fuel is unusable)
Use most economical cruise setting for maximum endurance
AFM gives both total and usable fuel capacity
Subtract reserves for practical usable endurance

syllabus_desc: Describe the techniques and procedures for setting heading.
concept: Setting heading is how you transition from the departure phase to the cruise navigation phase. After takeoff, climb to a safe altitude, then turn to your first planned heading. Set heading at a known point  -  ideally the aerodrome itself or a prominent nearby feature. Note the time (this is your ATD) and start your stopwatch or note the UTC time. Set the planned magnetic heading on your DI and check it against the compass. From this moment, you're navigating  -  all your ETAs and checkpoints run from this point.
exam_tip: Set heading at a known point (the departure aerodrome or nearby feature). Note the time (ATD), set the DI to your planned heading, start timing.
key_facts: Climb to a safe altitude before setting heading
Set heading at a known reference point
Record ATD (actual time of departure/set heading time)
Set planned magnetic heading on the DI
Cross-check DI against magnetic compass
Start timing  -  all ETAs run from this point

syllabus_desc: Describe the cruise routine / activity cycle.
concept: In cruise, you should develop a systematic cycle of activities  -  don't just stare at the horizon. A good routine: every few minutes, check heading (DI aligned with compass?), check altitude (maintaining planned level?), scan for traffic, check engine instruments (T's and P's), check fuel state, and do a map reading check (where am I? what should I see next?). Some instructors teach the acronym HASEL or similar, but the key is a regular cycle that keeps you situationally aware. Don't get fixated on one task  -  keep the cycle moving.
exam_tip: Systematic cruise cycle: heading check → altitude check → traffic scan → engine instruments → fuel → map reading. Don't fixate  -  keep the cycle moving.
key_facts: Heading: DI aligned with compass? On planned heading?
Altitude: maintaining planned cruising level?
Traffic: systematic external visual scan
Engine instruments: temperatures and pressures normal?
Fuel: fuel state vs plan, change tanks if needed
Map reading: where am I? What should I see next?

syllabus_desc: Describe the techniques for maintaining a flight log.
concept: Your flight log is your in-flight navigation record. At each checkpoint, record the ATA (actual time of arrival) and compare it with the ETA. Note any revised headings, wind changes, or ground speed corrections. Record fuel readings at regular intervals. If you amend an ETA, write the new one clearly. The flight log keeps you organised and prevents you from losing track of where you are in the flight. It's also your evidence for SARTIME calculations and post-flight debriefing. Keep entries brief but legible  -  you're flying an aircraft, not writing an essay.
exam_tip: Record ATA at each checkpoint. Compare with ETA. Note revised headings, GS, and fuel. Keep entries brief but legible  -  it's your in-flight management tool.
key_facts: Record ATA at each checkpoint
Compare ATA with ETA  -  note early/late
Record revised headings, GS, and ETAs
Log fuel state at regular intervals
Keep entries brief and legible
Use the log for SARTIME management

syllabus_desc: Describe the techniques for turning points.
