concept: At turning points, you need to positively identify the feature, note the ATA, then turn to the new heading for the next leg. Before reaching the turning point, brief yourself: what's the new heading? What's the first feature I should see on the next leg? At the turning point, note the time, turn to the new magnetic heading on the DI, and look for the first checkpoint on the new leg. If you can't positively identify the turning point, don't just turn at the ETA  -  circle and confirm your position first. A wrong turn compounds navigation errors.
exam_tip: At turning points: identify the feature, note ATA, turn to new heading, look for first feature on next leg. If uncertain of position, circle and confirm before turning.
key_facts: Positively identify the turning point feature
Record ATA at the turning point
Turn to the new magnetic heading
Brief the next leg: heading, first checkpoint, distance/time
If unsure of position, orbit and confirm before turning

syllabus_desc: Describe the techniques for approaching / rejoining at a destination aerodrome.
concept: As you approach your destination, listen to the ATIS (if available) or AWIS for the active runway, QNH, and weather. Plan your circuit join  -  in NZ, the standard join is overhead at circuit altitude + 500 ft (for an overhead join) or at 45° to the downwind leg. Identify the aerodrome and circuit direction. If unfamiliar with the aerodrome, review the AIPNZ Vol 4 entry beforehand. Make your inbound radio call at the appropriate distance. Set the QNH, complete pre-landing checks, and be prepared to adjust for traffic already in the circuit.
exam_tip: Before arrival: get ATIS/AWIS, plan circuit join (overhead or 45° to downwind), set QNH, make inbound radio call, review AIPNZ for unfamiliar aerodromes.
key_facts: Listen to ATIS/AWIS for active runway, QNH, weather
Plan circuit join: overhead join or 45° to downwind leg
Review AIPNZ Vol 4 for unfamiliar aerodrome procedures
Make inbound radio call at appropriate distance
Set QNH and complete pre-landing checks
Be prepared to fit in with existing circuit traffic
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, AIPNZ Vol 4, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Describe the techniques for map reading in flight.
concept: Map reading is the art of matching what you see outside to what's shown on the chart. The key technique: look at the chart first, predict what you should see ahead, then look outside for those features. Don't try to identify features you see and then find them on the chart  -  that's backwards and unreliable. Use large, distinctive features first (coastlines, major rivers, lakes, mountain ranges, cities), then refine with smaller features (roads, railway lines, smaller towns). Keep the chart oriented in the direction of travel. In NZ, coastlines and mountains are excellent references.
exam_tip: Look at chart first → predict what to see → look outside. Use large features first (coast, rivers, mountains), then smaller ones. Keep chart oriented to direction of travel.
key_facts: Chart first: predict what you should see ahead
Then look outside to confirm the features
Start with large features: coastlines, rivers, mountains, cities
Refine with smaller features: roads, railways, small towns
Keep chart oriented to your direction of travel
NZ: coastlines and mountain ranges are excellent references

syllabus_desc: Describe and apply techniques for position fixing.
concept: Position fixing confirms your exact location by identifying ground features and plotting your position on the chart. The simplest fix is flying over a known feature (town, airfield, lake). You can also use two or more position lines that intersect  -  for example, abeam a town (one line) and on a railway (second line). The more position lines you have, the more accurate your fix. Record the time of each fix on your flight log. Regular fixes are the backbone of VFR navigation  -  they reset your DR accuracy and let you correct for wind changes.
exam_tip: Fix by overflying known features or intersecting position lines. Record the time. Regular fixes keep your navigation accurate and reset DR errors.
key_facts: Overhead a known feature = simplest fix
Two or more intersecting position lines = accurate fix
Position lines: bearings from landmarks, railways, roads, rivers
Record time of every fix on the flight log
Fixes reset DR accuracy and reveal wind changes

syllabus_desc: Describe and apply techniques for changing heading to make good the desired track.
concept: If you discover you're off track, you need to change heading to regain your planned track. First, determine how far off track you are (visually, from the chart). Then use the 1:60 rule or drift lines to calculate the correction. Apply track error + closing angle to regain track by the next waypoint. Turn onto the new heading and monitor. If you're only slightly off, a small correction early is better than a large correction later. Don't just aim for the next waypoint and ignore the off-track error  -  you may miss important airspace or terrain clearance margins.
exam_tip: Determine off-track distance. Calculate TE + CA using 1:60 rule. Apply the correction and monitor. Small corrections early are better than large ones later.
key_facts: Determine how far off track using chart and ground references
Calculate TE + CA using 1:60 rule or drift lines
Apply total correction toward the desired track
Monitor after correction  -  check you're converging
Small early corrections are better than large late ones

syllabus_desc: Describe and apply techniques for changing heading to make good next turning point or destination.
concept: If you're off track and close to your turning point or destination, you may choose to simply alter heading directly toward the waypoint rather than regaining the planned track. Estimate the bearing to the waypoint from your current position (visually from the chart), convert to a magnetic heading accounting for wind, and fly direct. This 'short cut' works well when you're close and can see the waypoint. For longer corrections, regaining the planned track is usually better because it keeps you in known airspace and on your planned terrain clearance margins.
exam_tip: If close to the waypoint, alter heading direct to it rather than regaining the track. Estimate the bearing from your current position, apply wind correction, and fly direct.
key_facts: Alternative to regaining track: fly direct to the waypoint
Works best when close to the turning point or destination
Estimate bearing to waypoint from current position
Apply wind correction for the new heading
For longer legs, regaining track is usually preferred

syllabus_desc: Describe and apply techniques for amending ETA.
concept: When you arrive at a checkpoint early or late, revise your ETAs using the proportional method. If you're at the halfway point and 4 minutes early, you'll likely arrive about 4 minutes early at the destination  -  subtract 4 minutes from your ETA. If at the one-third point, the correction is about double the timing error. Record the amended ETA on your flight log. Pass the amended ETA to ATC if in controlled airspace (they need it for traffic management). Also recalculate your SARTIME if the destination ETA changes significantly.
exam_tip: Use the proportional method to revise ETAs. Update your flight log, pass changes to ATC if in controlled airspace, and adjust SARTIME if needed.
key_facts: Use proportional method: ETA change = (error × remaining) ÷ flown
Record amended ETA on flight log
Pass amended ETA to ATC in controlled airspace
Recalculate SARTIME if destination ETA changes significantly
Also reassess fuel state against revised timings

syllabus_desc: Calculate a heading to make good a reciprocal track.
concept: To fly back along your track (reciprocal), the heading is: reciprocal of your outbound heading ± double the drift. If outbound heading was 090°M with 5° starboard drift, the outbound track was 095°M. Reciprocal track = 275°M. On the return, the crosswind that caused starboard drift outbound will cause port drift inbound. So the inbound heading = 275° + 2×5° = 285°M (applying double drift to the windward side). Or simply: take the reciprocal of the heading, then apply double drift on the opposite side.
exam_tip: Reciprocal heading = reciprocal of outbound heading ± double the drift on the opposite side. The crosswind reverses, so drift doubles and flips.
key_facts: Reciprocal track = outbound track ± 180°
Inbound drift is opposite to outbound drift
Reciprocal heading = reciprocal of outbound heading ± 2× drift
Apply double drift to the windward side
Check with nav computer if time permits

syllabus_desc: Calculate an aircraft's position given bearing and distance from an identified ground position.
concept: If you can identify a ground feature and estimate the bearing and distance from it, you can plot your position. From the chart, find the ground feature. Apply the magnetic bearing FROM the feature (convert to true if needed) and measure the distance along that bearing using the latitude scale. That's your position. This technique is useful when you can identify a feature off to one side but aren't directly over anything identifiable. GPS makes this largely academic for everyday flying, but it's a fundamental skill and still examined.
exam_tip: Identify a ground feature, estimate bearing and distance from it, and plot on the chart. Convert magnetic bearing to true if plotting on the chart.
key_facts: Identify a ground feature on the chart
Estimate or measure the bearing FROM the feature
Estimate the distance using known references
Plot the position on the chart along that bearing and distance
Convert magnetic bearing to true for chart plotting

syllabus_desc: Describe the techniques and procedures for re-establishing position if lost or unsure of position.
concept: If you're uncertain of your position, don't panic  -  follow a systematic procedure. First, maintain heading and altitude (don't wander randomly). Check your last known position and calculate your DR position from there. Look for large, unmistakable features  -  coastlines, major rivers, mountain ranges, large towns. Climb if possible  -  higher altitude gives better visibility and radio range. If you have GPS, use it. If near a coast, fly toward it  -  coastlines are easy to follow and identify. Consider calling ATC for assistance  -  they can provide radar vectors or help you identify your position. The worst thing you can do is fly randomly.
exam_tip: Don't panic. Maintain heading and altitude. Look for big features (coast, rivers, mountains). Climb for better visibility. Use GPS if available. Call ATC for help.
key_facts: Maintain heading and altitude  -  don't wander
Calculate DR position from last known fix
Look for large unmistakable features (coast, rivers, mountains)
Climb for better visibility and radio range
Use GPS if available
Call ATC for assistance  -  they can radar-identify you
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Describe the techniques and procedures for diverting from the pre-planned route or destination.
concept: If you need to divert (weather, emergency, fuel concerns), you need to act quickly and methodically. Select an alternate aerodrome  -  preferably one you've already briefed on. Estimate the bearing and distance from your current position to the alternate using the chart. Apply wind correction mentally (use the 1:60 rule if needed). Calculate approximate flight time and fuel required. Advise ATC of your diversion. If you don't have time for precise calculations, just turn toward the alternate, fly, and refine en route. Getting to a safe aerodrome promptly matters more than perfect navigation.
exam_tip: Select alternate aerodrome. Estimate bearing and distance from current position. Apply approximate wind correction. Advise ATC. When in doubt, fly first, calculate second.
key_facts: Select suitable alternate (pre-briefed if possible)
Estimate bearing and distance from current position
Apply approximate wind correction for heading
Calculate approximate flight time and fuel required
Advise ATC of diversion
Safety first  -  get moving, refine calculations en route

syllabus_desc: Describe the techniques and procedures for navigating at low level when forced to do so by bad weather.
concept: Low-level navigation is demanding and risky. If weather forces you below your planned altitude: slow down (lower airspeed gives more reaction time), increase lookout for terrain and obstacles, use prominent line features for navigation (roads, railways, rivers, power lines), keep map reading simple (large features only  -  you can't identify small features at low level and high workload), and constantly reassess the weather. If conditions continue to deteriorate, turn back before you run out of options. In NZ, be acutely aware of terrain  -  many routes have rising ground and obstacles that aren't a factor at normal cruise altitude.
exam_tip: Slow down, increase lookout, follow line features (roads, rivers), simplify map reading. Constantly reassess weather. Turn back before options run out.
key_facts: Slow down  -  lower airspeed gives more reaction time
Increase lookout for terrain, obstacles, and wires
Follow prominent line features: roads, rivers, railways, coastlines
Simplify map reading  -  large features only
Constantly reassess weather  -  turn back if deteriorating
NZ terrain rises rapidly  -  know what's ahead and below
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Describe the techniques and procedures for navigating in mountainous terrain.
concept: NZ's mountains demand respect. When flying near or over ranges: always have an escape route planned, approach ridges at a 45° angle (so you can turn away quickly if conditions are bad on the other side), maintain extra altitude for safety margins, beware of mountain waves and severe turbulence on the lee side of ranges, expect stronger and unpredictable winds near peaks, watch for rapidly changing weather (conditions can deteriorate in minutes). The Kaimai Range between Waikato and Bay of Plenty, the Tararua Range near Wellington, and the entire Southern Alps all demand careful planning. Never fly over mountains into a cul-de-sac with no way out.
exam_tip: Approach ridges at 45° (easy escape). Extra altitude margins. Beware lee-side turbulence. Plan escape routes. NZ ranges demand respect  -  Kaimai, Tararua, Southern Alps.
key_facts: Approach ridges at 45° angle  -  easy turn-away if needed
Maintain extra altitude for safety margins
Beware mountain wave turbulence on lee side
Wind speed and direction are unpredictable near peaks
Weather can change rapidly in mountain areas
Never fly into a cul-de-sac with no escape route
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, CAR Part 91, AIPNZ ENR 5

syllabus_desc: Describe the requirements and procedures to manage SARTIME.
concept: SARTIME is your safety net. In NZ, when you file a flight plan, you nominate a SARTIME  -  the time by which you will have either arrived and closed your flight plan, or made contact to extend it. Default SARTIME = destination ETA + 30 minutes. You MUST close your flight plan before SARTIME expires  -  call ATC, Christchurch Information, or use the online system. If you're running late, call to extend your SARTIME before it expires. If you don't, search and rescue is activated, potentially involving aircraft, helicopters, and ground teams. Extending is easy  -  forgetting is not.
exam_tip: SARTIME = ETA + 30 min (default). MUST close or extend before it expires. Failing to do so activates SAR operations  -  expensive and wasteful. Extending is easy.
key_facts: SARTIME = destination ETA + 30 minutes (default in NZ)
Must close flight plan before SARTIME expires
Close by calling ATC, Christchurch Information, or online
If running late, extend SARTIME before it expires
Failure to close/extend activates SAR operations
Make SARTIME management part of your standard procedures
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, CAR Part 91.311, AIPNZ ENR 1.10

syllabus_desc: Describe the procedures and techniques to safely manage the fuel for a given flight.
concept: Good fuel management starts before the flight: calculate fuel required (trip + taxi + reserve + alternate), confirm you have enough usable fuel on board, and check fuel state visually or with dipstick during preflight. In flight: monitor fuel consumption at regular intervals (compare actual vs planned), switch tanks as required to maintain balance, note fuel remaining at each checkpoint, and continually assess whether you have enough fuel to reach your destination with legal reserves. If fuel state becomes marginal, divert early  -  don't press on hoping to make it. An early diversion for fuel is smart airmanship, not failure.
exam_tip: Pre-flight: calculate and confirm fuel. In-flight: monitor consumption, switch tanks, compare actual vs planned. If marginal, divert early  -  don't press on.
key_facts: Pre-flight: calculate total fuel required and confirm on board
Visual check or dipstick fuel during preflight inspection
In-flight: monitor consumption at each checkpoint
Switch tanks as required to maintain fuel balance
Compare actual consumption vs planned at checkpoints
If fuel state becomes marginal, divert early
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Explain the limitations of using GPS/GNSS to supplement normal visual navigation.
concept: GPS is a fantastic supplement to visual navigation, but it's not a replacement for map reading skills at PPL level. Limitations include: GPS can fail (battery, signal loss, satellite geometry), waypoint databases can be outdated, it's easy to select the wrong waypoint (especially in NZ where some place names are similar), GPS doesn't show terrain or obstacles, and relying solely on GPS can degrade your map reading skills. Over-reliance on GPS leads to 'head down' flying, reducing lookout and situational awareness. Use GPS to confirm your visual navigation, not to replace it.
exam_tip: GPS supplements visual nav  -  doesn't replace it. Limitations: can fail, databases can be outdated, wrong waypoint selection, reduces lookout if over-relied upon.
key_facts: GPS supplements visual navigation  -  doesn't replace it
Can fail: battery, signal loss, poor satellite geometry
Waypoint databases can be outdated or incorrect
Easy to select wrong waypoint  -  verify visually
GPS doesn't show terrain or obstacles
Over-reliance reduces map reading skills and lookout
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, CAR Part 91, AIPNZ ENR

syllabus_desc: Explain precautions when inserting data with the keypad.
concept: When entering waypoints or data into a GPS unit, always verify the data after entry. Check that the waypoint name, coordinates, and bearing/distance make sense. A common error is selecting the wrong waypoint from a list  -  NZ has places with similar names. Cross-reference the GPS-displayed position with your chart. Enter data before flight when possible, not while flying. If you must enter data in flight, do it in straight and level flight with a good lookout, and brief your plan to any passengers so they can help watch for traffic.
exam_tip: Always verify entered data against the chart. Enter waypoints before flight. Check for similar-named waypoints. Cross-reference GPS position with chart.
key_facts: Verify all entered data against your chart
Cross-check waypoint name, coordinates, and bearing/distance
Watch for similar-named waypoints
Enter data before flight when possible
If entering in flight, maintain lookout and straight/level flight

syllabus_desc: Explain precautions when operating/reading the unit while maintaining a proper lookout.
concept: The biggest risk with GPS is getting 'head down'  -  staring at the screen instead of looking outside. In a single-pilot VFR aircraft, your primary job is to see and avoid other traffic and maintain visual reference with the ground. Glance at the GPS briefly, then eyes back outside. Use the same technique as reading instruments in VFR flight  -  brief scans inside, long looks outside. Set up the GPS display so the information you need is visible at a glance. If the GPS requires complex button-pushing, wait until you're in stable cruise flight.
exam_tip: Brief glances at GPS, long looks outside. Same as instrument scanning. Avoid getting 'head down.' Set up the display for at-a-glance reading.
key_facts: Brief glances at GPS, long looks outside
Same principle as VFR instrument scanning
Avoid 'head down' flying  -  primary job is see-and-avoid
Set up display for at-a-glance information
Complex operations only during stable cruise flight

syllabus_desc: Explain precautions when operating/reading the unit in marginal weather conditions.
concept: In marginal weather, your workload is already high  -  navigation is harder, terrain clearance is critical, and you need maximum lookout. This is exactly when you'll be tempted to rely heavily on GPS, but also when you least afford to take your eyes off the outside world. Use GPS sparingly and for quick position checks only. Don't follow a GPS direct-to route if it takes you into terrain or cloud. Trust your visual references first  -  if you can't maintain VMC, no amount of GPS accuracy will keep you safe. Consider turning back while you still can.
exam_tip: In marginal weather, use GPS for quick position checks only. Don't let it distract from lookout. Don't follow GPS into terrain or cloud. Visual references first.
key_facts: Marginal weather = high workload  -  minimise GPS interaction
Quick position checks only  -  eyes outside
Don't follow GPS direct-to into terrain or cloud
Visual references and terrain clearance take priority
Consider turning back before conditions worsen further

syllabus_desc: State the factors influencing GPS/GNSS reliability.
concept: GPS reliability depends on several factors: number of satellites visible (need minimum 4 for 3D position), satellite geometry (PDOP  -  poor geometry gives less accurate fixes), signal interference (terrain masking in valleys, electronic interference), ionospheric and atmospheric conditions, and the quality of the GPS receiver itself. In NZ, mountain terrain can mask satellites in valleys, reducing accuracy. RAIM (Receiver Autonomous Integrity Monitoring) alerts you if GPS accuracy is degraded. Always have a backup plan  -  if GPS fails mid-flight, you need to be able to navigate visually.
exam_tip: GPS reliability depends on: satellite number and geometry, signal masking (valleys/terrain), interference, atmospheric conditions, receiver quality. Always have a visual nav backup.
key_facts: Need minimum 4 satellites for 3D position fix
Satellite geometry (PDOP) affects accuracy
Terrain masking in valleys can block satellite signals
Ionospheric conditions affect signal accuracy
RAIM alerts if integrity is compromised
Always maintain visual navigation as backup
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, AIPNZ ENR

syllabus_desc: State the two types of radar currently used in New Zealand.
concept: New Zealand uses two types of radar: Primary Surveillance Radar (PSR) and Secondary Surveillance Radar (SSR). Primary radar works by sending out radio pulses and detecting reflections off aircraft  -  it sees everything, but gives only position (range and bearing). Secondary radar interrogates the aircraft's transponder and receives a coded reply  -  this gives position plus identification (squawk code) and, in Mode C/S, altitude information. Most NZ radar facilities use a combination of both, but SSR provides the richer information that ATC relies on for traffic separation.
exam_tip: Two types: Primary Surveillance Radar (PSR  -  detects reflections) and Secondary Surveillance Radar (SSR  -  interrogates transponder). SSR provides more information.
key_facts: Primary Surveillance Radar (PSR): detects radar reflections from aircraft
PSR gives range and bearing only  -  no identification or altitude
Secondary Surveillance Radar (SSR): interrogates aircraft transponder
SSR gives identification (squawk code) and altitude (Mode C/S)
NZ ATC uses combined PSR and SSR
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, AIPNZ ENR 1.6

syllabus_desc: Describe the method of operation of each type of radar.
concept: Primary radar transmits a pulse of radio energy that bounces off the aircraft and returns to the antenna  -  the time delay gives the range, and the antenna direction gives the bearing. It works regardless of whether the aircraft has a transponder. Secondary radar sends an interrogation signal at 1030 MHz; the aircraft's transponder receives this and replies on 1090 MHz with a coded signal containing the squawk code and altitude. SSR requires a working transponder on the aircraft  -  without one, only primary radar can see you. SSR is more accurate, has longer range, and provides identification.
exam_tip: PSR: transmit pulse → detect reflection → range and bearing. SSR: interrogate transponder at 1030 MHz → reply at 1090 MHz with code and altitude.
key_facts: PSR: transmit radio pulse, detect reflection from aircraft
PSR: time delay = range, antenna direction = bearing
PSR works without any aircraft equipment
SSR: interrogation signal at 1030 MHz
SSR: transponder reply at 1090 MHz with code and altitude
SSR requires a functioning transponder on the aircraft
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, AIPNZ ENR 1.6

syllabus_desc: Explain what is meant by transponder Mode A and Mode C.
concept: Mode A is the basic transponder mode  -  it transmits your four-digit squawk code when interrogated by SSR. This tells ATC who you are (or at least which code you're squawking). Mode C adds pressure altitude information  -  the transponder sends your squawk code plus your altitude from the encoding altimeter. Mode C (or 'ALT' on the transponder) is what you should normally use in NZ, as it gives ATC your altitude along with your identification. Without Mode C, ATC can see your position but not your altitude, which limits the services they can provide.
exam_tip: Mode A = squawk code only. Mode C = squawk code + pressure altitude. Use Mode C (ALT) as standard in NZ.
key_facts: Mode A: transmits squawk code only (identification)
Mode C: transmits squawk code + pressure altitude
Mode C uses the encoding altimeter for altitude data
Use Mode C (ALT position) as standard in NZ
Without Mode C, ATC cannot see your altitude
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, AIPNZ ENR 1.6, CAR Part 91.513

syllabus_desc: List and explain radar services that may be available to VFR flights.
concept: In NZ, VFR flights can receive several radar services when in range of radar coverage. Radar Information Service provides traffic information  -  ATC tells you about other aircraft in your vicinity. Radar monitoring means ATC is watching your progress and will alert you to potential issues. In some cases, ATC may provide radar vectors (specific headings to fly) for traffic avoidance or to help you join controlled airspace. These services are available on request and subject to ATC workload. Not all of NZ has radar coverage  -  particularly remote and mountainous areas. Squawk 1200 on Mode C to be radar-visible.
exam_tip: VFR radar services: traffic information, radar monitoring, and sometimes vectors. Available on request, workload permitting. Squawk 1200 Mode C to be visible.
key_facts: Radar Information Service: traffic information about nearby aircraft
Radar monitoring: ATC watches your progress
Radar vectors: specific headings for traffic avoidance
Available on request, subject to ATC workload
Not all NZ has radar coverage (gaps in remote/mountain areas)
Squawk 1200 on Mode C (ALT) for radar visibility
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, AIPNZ ENR 1.6, CAR Part 91
